EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon and early evening thunderstorms focused along a weak surface boundary from southwest Minnesota into areas south of Interstate 90 in southeast South Dakota.  Even with very weak deep-layer shear, modest surface-based instability of 1500-2000 J/kg led to the rapid development of storms, which peaked in mainly hail producing severity early in the life cycle, as well as an erratic tornado in eastern Bon Homme County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system passing south and east of the region brought freezing drizzle and freezing rain to south-central, north-central, and northeastern South Dakota, causing icy roadways. Numerous accidents occurred due to the icy roads, especially along Interstate 90 in Jones county. The South Dakota Department of Transportation issued a no-travel advised in central South Dakota around 10 AM on January 16th. A few semi-tractor trailers jackknifed, causing temporary road closures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Marshall County Emergency Management reported slick roadways in the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An Arctic high pressure brought cold temperatures and bitterly cold wind chills the night of January 29th through the morning hours on January 30th. The combination of low temperatures from the teens below zero to 25 degrees below zero and winds of 10 to 15 mph produced wind chill values of 35 below zero to 47 degrees below zero. Some of the coldest wind chills include 47 below 7 miles south of Claremont, 46 below 10 miles west of Long Lake, 46 below 1 west-northwest of Summit, and 45 below 10 miles southwest of Long Lake and 7 miles north of Frederick.

A strong upper level wave lifted out of the southwestern U.S. and closed off moving across Iowa by early on January 19. A band of snow developed within the leading warm advection zone from northern Nebraska to northern Iowa by late afternoon on January 18, and with unstable conditions near the frontal axis, the band evolved into a more narrow zone of intense snowfall into the overnight hours. Dry air poised to the north created a sharp gradient to snowfall on the north side of the band.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled 5.8 inches at White Lake and 3.5 inches 4 miles northwest of Stickney.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Overall road conditions were poor due to drifting snow with light snowfall and gusty winds, which resulted in several accidents and an indirect fatality.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very light snowfall during the early morning was accompanied and followed by gusty northwest wind, which resulted in drifting of snow and the formation of black ice on roadways. A vehicle lost control on ice-covered Interstate 29 near mile marker 71, entered the median and rolled, which resulted in an indirect fatality to the driver and injury to a passenger.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Periods of heavy snowfall buried much of southeast South Dakota on January 2-3. The first band of heavy snow developed on the morning of January 2, narrowing as it lifted north and locked in along a Pickstown to Dell Rapids line. This band broadened with lesser rates through the night, but convective development resulted in a secondary period of heavier snowfall (with rates up to 2 to 3 inches per hour) within the deformation zone northwest of the upper level low across southeast South Dakota and southwest Minnesota during the early morning to midday hours on January 3.  Areas that received both heavy bands saw two-day totals of 20 to 27 inches from Lake Andes to Alexandria to Dell Rapids. Several one and two day county snowfall records were established. Roads were closed or impassable during the height of the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snowfall occurred with a two-day total of 27.0 inches 2 miles north of Lake Andes, 22.5 inches at Wagner, 20.0 inches at Platte, and 19.0 inches 2 miles northeast of Academy.

Low pressure intensified and moved from the Central Plains into Minnesota. Initially, warm thermal profiles allowed for mixed precipitation or all rain. However, as winds shifted to the northwest, colder air rapidly changed precipitation over to snow. While additional accumulation of snow was fairly light (up to 1 to 3 inches in areas of greatest snowfall along an axis from Tyndall to Salem to Minneota), northwest winds were bolstered to a gusty 40 to 60 mph and resulted in widespread blowing snow along with spotty blizzard conditions near the Buffalo Ridge. Travel was not recommended during the overnight and early morning hours across parts of southwest Minnesota and east central South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong northwest winds were sustained from 30 to 38 mph, with gusts as high as 55 mph at the Automated Surface Observing System site KHON at Huron, 52 mph at South Dakota Road Weather Information System site near Wolsey, and 51 mph near Cavour.

A band of snow developed within the leading warm advection zone from northern Nebraska to northern Iowa by late afternoon on January 18, and with unstable conditions near the frontal axis, the band evolved into a more narrow zone of intense snowfall into the overnight hours. Dry air poised to the north created a sharp gradient to snowfall on the north side of the band.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled 9.0 inches 9 miles north of Gregory and 9 miles south of Dallas, 8.5 inches 6 miles south-southwest of Herrick, 7.4 inches 4 miles south-southwest of Pickstown, and 7.0 inches at Gregory.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With colder air entrenched across the northern tier of the U.S., a jet streak dropped southeast out of Canada and a strong zone of frontogenetic forcing along the boundary of colder air resulted in a band of heavy snowfall, with brief rates up to 1 to 2 inches per hour through the Missouri River corridor. Winds were not excessive with the system, with no significant blowing and drifting impacts.  Snowfall generally reached 6 to 12 inches along the Missouri River, but dry air poised to the north rapidly dropped amounts to an inch or less north of Interstate 90.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled 3.0 inches at Wessington Springs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Periods of heavy snowfall buried much of southeast South Dakota on January 2-3. The first band of heavy snow developed on the morning of January 2, narrowing as it lifted north and locked in along a Pickstown to Dell Rapids line. This band broadened with lesser rates through the night, but convective development resulted in a secondary period of heavier snowfall (with rates up to 2 to 3 inches per hour) within the deformation zone northwest of the upper level low across southeast South Dakota and southwest Minnesota during the early morning to midday hours on January 3.  Areas that received both heavy bands saw two-day totals of 20 to 27 inches from Lake Andes to Alexandria to Dell Rapids. Several one and two day county snowfall records were established. Roads were closed or impassable during the height of the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snowfall occurred with a two-day total of 12.8 inches at Tyndall and 8.0 inches 8 miles west-northwest of Springfield.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An Arctic high pressure brought cold temperatures and bitterly cold wind chills the night of January 29th through the morning hours on January 30th. The combination of low temperatures from the teens below zero to 25 degrees below zero and winds of 10 to 15 mph produced wind chill values of 35 below zero to 47 degrees below zero. Some of the coldest wind chills include 47 below 7 miles south of Claremont, 46 below 10 miles west of Long Lake, 46 below 1 west-northwest of Summit, and 45 below 10 miles southwest of Long Lake and 7 miles north of Frederick.

A long-duration winter storm developed across southeast South Dakota on February 21, with waves of heavier snowfall across the region as winds increased to a gusty 30 to 50 mph.  Blizzard conditions developed during the afternoon of February 22, with strong winds lingering into the midday hours of February 23.  During the event, widespread blowing and drifting snowfall led to the closure of Interstate 90, as well as Interstate 29 north of Sioux Falls.  Travel was not recommended in numerous counties, with schools and county/state offices closed due to the hazardous conditions. Total snowfall varied from around 3 inches in extreme southeast South Dakota to around 17 inches near Huron.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Over the two-day period, snowfall accumulated to 10.1 inches at Hurley and 8.5 inches at Marion.  Whiteout conditions became widespread, especially in rural areas, due to winds which peaked at 41 mph near Parker.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system tracked across the western United States and through the central Plains, bringing snow to much of southwestern and south central South Dakota. The heaviest snow developed across far southern South Dakota, where eight to 16 inches were reported in many areas, with locally higher amounts across far south central South Dakota. Amounts dropped off sharply to the north, with little or no snow across northwestern South Dakota.

A storm system passing south and east of the region brought freezing drizzle and freezing rain to south-central, north-central, and northeastern South Dakota, causing icy roadways. Numerous accidents occurred due to the icy roads, especially along Interstate 90 in Jones county. The South Dakota Department of Transportation issued a no-travel advised in central South Dakota around 10 AM on January 16th. A few semi-tractor trailers jackknifed, causing temporary road closures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The South Dakota Highway Patrol reported U.S. Highway 12 was pure ice. Several vehicles slid into ditches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure intensified and moved from the Central Plains into Minnesota. Initially, warm thermal profiles allowed for mixed precipitation or all rain. However, as winds shifted to the northwest, colder air rapidly changed precipitation over to snow. While additional accumulation of snow was fairly light (up to 1 to 3 inches in areas of greatest snowfall along an axis from Tyndall to Salem to Minneota), northwest winds were bolstered to a gusty 40 to 60 mph and resulted in widespread blowing snow along with spotty blizzard conditions near the Buffalo Ridge. Travel was not recommended during the overnight and early morning hours across parts of southwest Minnesota and east central South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Northwest wind was sustained from 35 to 45 mph, with gusts as high as 61 mph at the South Dakota Road Weather Information System site SD554 10 miles west of Platte.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long-duration winter storm developed across southeast South Dakota on February 21, with waves of heavier snowfall across the region as winds increased to a gusty 30 to 50 mph.  Blizzard conditions developed during the afternoon of February 22, with strong winds lingering into the midday hours of February 23.  During the event, widespread blowing and drifting snowfall led to the closure of Interstate 90, as well as Interstate 29 north of Sioux Falls.  Travel was not recommended in numerous counties, with schools and county/state offices closed due to the hazardous conditions. Total snowfall varied from around 3 inches in extreme southeast South Dakota to around 17 inches near Huron.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Over the two-day period, snowfall accumulated to 10.3 inches 10 miles west of Dimock.  Whiteout conditions became widespread, especially in rural areas, due to winds which peaked at 41 mph near Corsica.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With colder air entrenched across the northern tier of the U.S., a jet streak dropped southeast out of Canada and a strong zone of frontogenetic forcing along the boundary of colder air resulted in a band of heavy snowfall, with brief rates up to 1 to 2 inches per hour through the Missouri River corridor. Winds were not excessive with the system, with no significant blowing and drifting impacts.  Snowfall generally reached 6 to 12 inches along the Missouri River, but dry air poised to the north rapidly dropped amounts to an inch or less north of Interstate 90.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled 11.3 inches at Wagner, 10.0 inches 5 miles south of Wagner, 8.0 inches 2 miles north of Lake Andes, and 6.1 inches 2 miles northeast of Academy.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system tracked across the western United States and through the central Plains, bringing snow to much of southwestern and south central South Dakota. The heaviest snow developed across far southern South Dakota, where eight to 16 inches were reported in many areas, with locally higher amounts across far south central South Dakota. Amounts dropped off sharply to the north, with little or no snow across northwestern South Dakota.

An area of low pressure moved across Kansas, Iowa, and into Minnesota from January 2nd through the 4th. The storm system spread accumulating snowfall, mainly along I-90 in south central South Dakota and into extreme eastern parts of South Dakota. A dry area of high pressure over North Dakota severely limited snowfall amounts over central and north-central locations. Some snowfall amounts include 10 inches 4 miles west of Iona, 7.1 inches 4 miles west-northwest Lake Cochrane Recreation, and 6 inches at Milbank. A few schools were delayed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Periods of heavy snowfall buried much of southeast South Dakota on January 2-3. The first band of heavy snow developed on the morning of January 2, narrowing as it lifted north and locked in along a Pickstown to Dell Rapids line. This band broadened with lesser rates through the night, but convective development resulted in a secondary period of heavier snowfall (with rates up to 2 to 3 inches per hour) within the deformation zone northwest of the upper level low across southeast South Dakota and southwest Minnesota during the early morning to midday hours on January 3.  Areas that received both heavy bands saw two-day totals of 20 to 27 inches from Lake Andes to Alexandria to Dell Rapids. Several one and two day county snowfall records were established. Roads were closed or impassable during the height of the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A historic snowfall pummeled the area with two distinct periods of heavy snowfall, which totaled as much as 18 to 27 inches in the northern portion of the county, including 26.5 inches 4 miles west-northwest of Dell Rapids, 25.5 inches at Humboldt, and 23.3 inches 4 miles north-northwest of Renner. Snow fell at a rate of 2 to 3 inches per hour at times during the afternoon of January 2 and the morning of January 3. Schools and government offices were closed, and the Sioux Falls Airport ceased operations for almost 24 hours. Over 190 vehicles were stranded on Sioux Falls city streets, along with 27 non-injury accidents and 3 indirect injury accidents.

The heaviest snow fell across south central South Dakota, where anywhere from six inches to around a foot of accumulation was noted over portions of Todd and Tripp Counties. Elsewhere across far southern South Dakota, two to five inches of snow was reported in many areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long-duration winter storm developed across southeast South Dakota on February 21, with waves of heavier snowfall across the region as winds increased to a gusty 30 to 50 mph.  Blizzard conditions developed during the afternoon of February 22, with strong winds lingering into the midday hours of February 23.  During the event, widespread blowing and drifting snowfall led to the closure of Interstate 90, as well as Interstate 29 north of Sioux Falls.  Travel was not recommended in numerous counties, with schools and county/state offices closed due to the hazardous conditions. Total snowfall varied from around 3 inches in extreme southeast South Dakota to around 17 inches near Huron.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Over the two-day period, snowfall accumulated to 13.0 inches at Madison.  Whiteout conditions became widespread, especially in rural areas, due to winds which peaked at 44 mph at Madison. The weight of the resulting 10 to 15 foot snow drifts snapped a gas line near the meter, which resulted in an explosion that destroyed one home and damaged another. There were three indirect injuries in the incident.

A clipper system moved from southern Saskatchewan into northern Minnesota from the afternoon hours of January 26th through the morning hours of January 27th. The clipper system brought a light wintry mix of precipitation and warm temperatures, with many locations reaching the mid-30s, to near 40 degrees. The warm temperatures limited blowing snow issues. However, the clipper system also produced high northwesterly winds with gusts of 55 to 63 mph reported. The winds and a glaze of ice on roadways caused the South Dakota Department of Transportation to issue a no-travel advised across northeastern South Dakota. Some of the highest wind gusts include 63 mph 4 miles west of Trail City, 62 mph 5 miles west-northwest of McLaughlin, and 61 mph 1 mile west-northwest of Summit.

Periods of heavy snowfall buried much of southeast South Dakota on January 2-3. The first band of heavy snow developed on the morning of January 2, narrowing as it lifted north and locked in along a Pickstown to Dell Rapids line. This band broadened with lesser rates through the night, but convective development resulted in a secondary period of heavier snowfall (with rates up to 2 to 3 inches per hour) within the deformation zone northwest of the upper level low across southeast South Dakota and southwest Minnesota during the early morning to midday hours on January 3.  Areas that received both heavy bands saw two-day totals of 20 to 27 inches from Lake Andes to Alexandria to Dell Rapids. Several one and two day county snowfall records were established. Roads were closed or impassable during the height of the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snowfall occurred with a two-day total of 12.0 inches 4 miles north-northeast of Forestburg and 10.7 inches near Woonsocket.

A storm system passing south and east of the region brought freezing drizzle and freezing rain to south-central, north-central, and northeastern South Dakota, causing icy roadways. Numerous accidents occurred due to the icy roads, especially along Interstate 90 in Jones county. The South Dakota Department of Transportation issued a no-travel advised in central South Dakota around 10 AM on January 16th. A few semi-tractor trailers jackknifed, causing temporary road closures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Lyman County Sheriff's Office reported several vehicles in the ditch due to icy road conditions along Interstate 90 from west of Presho to the Jones County line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure passing south and east of the region brought wintry weather to south-central South Dakota. While snowfall amounts were 6 inches or less, snow-covered roadways significantly impacted travel. Patchy freezing drizzle also caused travel issues. The worst travel conditions were along Interstate 90 across Jones and Lyman counties, where numerous vehicles slid into ditches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Jones County Emergency Manager reported  Interstate 90 was iced-covered, resulting in traffic accidents.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure intensified and moved from the Central Plains into Minnesota. Initially, warm thermal profiles allowed for mixed precipitation or all rain. However, as winds shifted to the northwest, colder air rapidly changed precipitation over to snow. While additional accumulation of snow was fairly light (up to 1 to 3 inches in areas of greatest snowfall along an axis from Tyndall to Salem to Minneota), northwest winds were bolstered to a gusty 40 to 60 mph and resulted in widespread blowing snow along with spotty blizzard conditions near the Buffalo Ridge. Travel was not recommended during the overnight and early morning hours across parts of southwest Minnesota and east central South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Northwest wind gusted as high as 53 mph at South Dakota Road Weather Information System site SD597 near Howard. With snowfall up to 1.5 inches 2 miles west of Vilas, the winds whipped up considerable blowing snow with several hours of visibility less than one mile.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An Arctic high pressure brought cold temperatures and bitterly cold wind chills the night of January 29th through the morning hours on January 30th. The combination of low temperatures from the teens below zero to 25 degrees below zero and winds of 10 to 15 mph produced wind chill values of 35 below zero to 47 degrees below zero. Some of the coldest wind chills include 47 below 7 miles south of Claremont, 46 below 10 miles west of Long Lake, 46 below 1 west-northwest of Summit, and 45 below 10 miles southwest of Long Lake and 7 miles north of Frederick.

A long-duration winter storm developed across southeast South Dakota on February 21, with waves of heavier snowfall across the region as winds increased to a gusty 30 to 50 mph.  Blizzard conditions developed during the afternoon of February 22, with strong winds lingering into the midday hours of February 23.  During the event, widespread blowing and drifting snowfall led to the closure of Interstate 90, as well as Interstate 29 north of Sioux Falls.  Travel was not recommended in numerous counties, with schools and county/state offices closed due to the hazardous conditions. Total snowfall varied from around 3 inches in extreme southeast South Dakota to around 17 inches near Huron.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Over the two-day period, snowfall accumulated to 13.2 inches at Mitchell.  Whiteout conditions became widespread, especially in rural areas, due to winds which peaked at 38 mph at Mitchell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system that tracked across North Dakota sent a cold front across the Dakotas and western Minnesota on the evening of February 8th through the early morning of February 9th. Winds gusted as high as 56 mph in Summit, 55 mph in Brandt and 53 mph in Watertown. Additionally, a light skiff of snow accompanied the system. The combination of strong winds on top of icy roads, recent light snow and a deep snowpack resulted in pockets of near blizzard conditions and hazardous travel in the higher elevations of the Sisseton Hills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A rollover was reported near Bristol with localized whiteout conditions and icy roads around noon on the 9th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A clipper low pressure system tracked across North Dakota into western Minnesota through the daytime of February 20th. It generated light snowfall of an inch or less for parts of northeast South Dakota, and then strong winds. Wind gusts of 40 to 50 mph were widespread across northeast South Dakota with a peak wind of 52 mph measured at the DOT site at Craven Corner between Aberdeen and Ipswich. 51 mph wind gusts were measured at Peever, Frederick and Summit as well. ||Blowing and drifting snow across icy roadways had a significant impact on travel, and sheriff's offices in Roberts and Grant counties advised no travel. Incidents of vehicles getting stuck in the ditch were reported across Roberts, Grant, Marshall, Day and Codington counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm chaser reports multiple vehicles in the ditch with low visibility along with drifting snow and ice.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A northwest to southeast band of snow moved east-northeast and affected mainly north central and northeast South Dakota during the mid-day hours on February 21st. Winds topped out between 25 and 35 mph, which was enough to cause some blowing and drifting snow. The resultant hazardous travel conditions were responsible for at least one multi-vehicle accident on interstate 29. Due to these deteriorating conditions, and as a precaution in anticipation of a second, stronger system following later that night into Wednesday, February 22nd, Interstate 29 was closed early from Watertown to the North Dakota state line. No travel was advised by the DOT after 6pm across all of northeastern South Dakota thereafter. Clark topped out at 6 inches with this system, while Milbank and Faulkton measured 5 inches. This was also followed by much colder air with wind chill values falling to around 35 degrees below zero in north central and northeast South Dakota.

Heavy snow and strong winds developed across the region associated with a classic Colorado Low type storm system around mid-day Wednesday, February 22, with strong winds lingering into the early morning hours of Thursday, February 23rd. The highest total of 9.6 inches was observed 4 miles west-northwest of the Lake Cochrane State Recreational Area, with 8.5 inches observed in LaBolt, and 8 inches reported 6 miles east of Hayes. Official observations from Pierre, Watertown and Aberdeen were 7.5, 7.5 and 7.2 inches respectively.  A peak wind gust of 56 mph was recorded at Macs Corner, and 54 mph at Summit, and many other locations experienced peak gusts between 40 and 50 mph.||Between the afternoon of Wednesday, February 22nd and Thursday, February 23rd, wind chills fell as low as 41 below zero in Eureka, and 40 below zero in Ridgeview and Selby, while most of the rest of central, north central and northeast South Dakota experienced wind chills of between 30 below zero to 40 below zero. ||Travel was severely impacted by this system. The South Dakota Department of Transportation had already closed Interstate 29 on February 21st, and closed Interstate 90 on February 22nd, in addition to advising no travel for just about every route east of Highway 83. Additionally, law enforcement in 10 counties also issued no travel advised during the course of the storm. It took well into Thursday, February 23rd to open many of these roads as many of them had drifted shut. ||In the midst of the storm, with moderate intensity snow, winds of 30 mph and temperatures around zero producing wind chills of 25 below zero, Mobridge fire department was dispatched to fight a structure fire.

A strong winter storm impacted the area, bringing significant snowfall, gusty winds, and a surge of Arctic air. A series of upper-level disturbances associated with the system overrode the arctic air at the surface and produced snowfall for an extended period. The Arctic cold front dropped temperatures to near or below zero for an extended period. Snowfall amounts were in the six to 12 inch range across much of western and south central South Dakota, with locally higher amounts. Amounts tapered off to three to six inches across far northwestern South Dakota. Winds gusting to 30 to 40 mph at times on the plains produced mostly minor blowing and drifting of snow. Wind chill values dropped to 20 below to 35 below zero during the event.

A low pressure system moved quickly across South Dakota and rapidly deepened as it tracked into Minnesota and the Western Great Lakes region. Little to no snow was observed west of the James Valley, and the main impact from the system was high winds, with peak gusts of 63 mph measured at the DOT sites in Ridgeview and Mellette, while the Aberdeen regional airport clocked a peak gust of 62 mph. Almost every observation site between north central and central South Dakota and down the James Valley had a peak wind gust of 50 mph or greater. Farther east, snow and high winds combined to produce blizzard conditions.

Breezy and very cold conditions moved in with Arctic high pressure after the departure of the previous day's winter storm. Temperatures fell to around 20 degrees below zero, and wind chills of ranged anywhere from 35 to 45 degrees below zero. The coldest recorded wind chill Friday, February 24th was 45 degrees below zero 9 miles north northeast of Groton, while Bowdle recorded a wind chill of 43 degrees below zero.

Heavy snow and strong winds developed across the region associated with a classic Colorado Low type storm system around mid-day Wednesday, February 22, with strong winds lingering into the early morning hours of Thursday, February 23rd. The highest total of 9.6 inches was observed 4 miles west-northwest of the Lake Cochrane State Recreational Area, with 8.5 inches observed in LaBolt, and 8 inches reported 6 miles east of Hayes. Official observations from Pierre, Watertown and Aberdeen were 7.5, 7.5 and 7.2 inches respectively.  A peak wind gust of 56 mph was recorded at Macs Corner, and 54 mph at Summit, and many other locations experienced peak gusts between 40 and 50 mph.||Between the afternoon of Wednesday, February 22nd and Thursday, February 23rd, wind chills fell as low as 41 below zero in Eureka, and 40 below zero in Ridgeview and Selby, while most of the rest of central, north central and northeast South Dakota experienced wind chills of between 30 below zero to 40 below zero. ||Travel was severely impacted by this system. The South Dakota Department of Transportation had already closed Interstate 29 on February 21st, and closed Interstate 90 on February 22nd, in addition to advising no travel for just about every route east of Highway 83. Additionally, law enforcement in 10 counties also issued no travel advised during the course of the storm. It took well into Thursday, February 23rd to open many of these roads as many of them had drifted shut. ||In the midst of the storm, with moderate intensity snow, winds of 30 mph and temperatures around zero producing wind chills of 25 below zero, Mobridge fire department was dispatched to fight a structure fire.

A compact and quick moving system brought snowfall to portions of northwestern South Dakota and the northern Black Hills. A band of heavy snow developed and passed across portions of Perkins, northern Meade, and Ziebach Counties during the evening, producing two to six inches of snow. Several inches of snow also fell across the higher elevations of the northern Black Hills during the late afternoon hours.

A low pressure system moved quickly across South Dakota and rapidly deepened as it tracked into Minnesota and the Western Great Lakes region. Little to no snow was observed west of the James Valley, and the main impact from the system was high winds, with peak gusts of 63 mph measured at the DOT sites in Ridgeview and Mellette, while the Aberdeen regional airport clocked a peak gust of 62 mph. Almost every observation site between north central and central South Dakota and down the James Valley had a peak wind gust of 50 mph or greater. Farther east, snow and high winds combined to produce blizzard conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved quickly across South Dakota and rapidly deepened as it tracked into Minnesota and the Western Great Lakes region. An initial rain/snow mix on the morning of Tuesday, February 14th transitioned to all snow between 4 and 5pm. Snow proceeded to continue through the very early morning hours of Wednesday, February 15th. The greatest reported accumulations include 4.5 inches 2 miles southeast of Roslyn, 3.8 inches in Clark, and 3.6 inches 3 miles west of Sisseton. Meanwhile, locations like Aberdeen only received a trace of snowfall and little to no snow was observed west of the James Valley. ||Winds with the system were quite strong, with a peak wind gust of 63 mph measured in Summit, and a peak gust of 62 mph near Clark and Milbank. Almost every observation site in northeastern South Dakota had a peak wind gust of 50 mph or greater with this system.||Interstate 29 was closed from Watertown north to the state line in preparation for deteriorating travel conditions on the evening of the 14th.  As expected, the combination of high winds and fresh snow resulted in blizzard conditions which persisted from the evening of the 14th through most of the morning of the 15th. With reports of multiple stranded vehicles, the Department of Transportation and several sheriff's offices advised no travel across the Sisseton Hills region during the event.

A pocket of D2/Severe Drought remains in place to start the month of February in north central South Dakota. The area received near normal precipitation for the month thanks to a series of systems, with Mobridge registering a slightly above normal 0.77 inches of liquid equivalent, while SDSU Mesonet and COOP observers also in the area of D2/Severe Drought such as Bowdle, McIntosh and McLaughlin actually showed a slight deficit.  Overall there was only a slight reduction of D2/Severe Drought coverage by the end of the month. This area has been in D2/Severe Drought since early November.

A clipper low pressure system tracked across North Dakota into western Minnesota through the daytime of February 20th. It generated light snowfall of an inch or less for parts of northeast South Dakota, and then strong winds. Wind gusts of 40 to 50 mph were widespread across northeast South Dakota with a peak wind of 52 mph measured at the DOT site at Craven Corner between Aberdeen and Ipswich. 51 mph wind gusts were measured at Peever, Frederick and Summit as well. ||Blowing and drifting snow across icy roadways had a significant impact on travel, and sheriff's offices in Roberts and Grant counties advised no travel. Incidents of vehicles getting stuck in the ditch were reported across Roberts, Grant, Marshall, Day and Codington counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Marshall county sheriff reported low visibility with drifted and icy roads as well as a stranded vehicles.

A favorable setup for isolated strong winds on the eastern side of the Prairie Coteau developed during the morning hours of February 25th, including a wind gust of 47 mph at Peever. Blowing snow and hazardous travel conditions ensued for those favored downslope locations including along Interstate 29. Low visibility and icy roads resulted in at least one jackknifed semi on the interstate near Wilmot.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway patrol assisted a jackknifed semi on Interstate 29. The COOP observer in Summit also reported ground blizzard conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front and upper level disturbance brought snow and gusty winds to much of western South Dakota. Most areas on the plains received an inch or less of snow, but much of the northern Black Hills area saw three to six inches of snow, especially the higher elevations. Wind gusts of 40 to 55 mph produced areas of blowing snow and reduced visibility at times, especially during the late afternoon and evening hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm impacted the area, bringing significant snowfall, gusty winds, and a surge of Arctic air. A series of upper-level disturbances associated with the system overrode the arctic air at the surface and produced snowfall for an extended period. The Arctic cold front dropped temperatures to near or below zero for an extended period. Snowfall amounts were in the six to 12 inch range across much of western and south central South Dakota, with locally higher amounts. Amounts tapered off to three to six inches across far northwestern South Dakota. Winds gusting to 30 to 40 mph at times on the plains produced mostly minor blowing and drifting of snow. Wind chill values dropped to 20 below to 35 below zero during the event.

A pocket of D2/Severe Drought remains in place to start the month of February in north central South Dakota. The area received near normal precipitation for the month thanks to a series of systems, with Mobridge registering a slightly above normal 0.77 inches of liquid equivalent, while SDSU Mesonet and COOP observers also in the area of D2/Severe Drought such as Bowdle, McIntosh and McLaughlin actually showed a slight deficit.  Overall there was only a slight reduction of D2/Severe Drought coverage by the end of the month. This area has been in D2/Severe Drought since early November.

A rapidly strengthening storm developed across the Northern Plains and into the western Great Lakes on March 16-17. An initial band of precipitation developed during the early morning hours of March 16 from eastern South Dakota into southwestern Minnesota. While precipitation started in many locations as light rain or brief light freezing rain, a sharp fall in temperatures in the morning hours forced a change to a couple hours of heavy snowfall, accompanied by increasing northerly wind. With most ditches already full and snow depths the morning of March 16 between 5 and 15 inches near and north of Interstate 90, the new snowfall and winds which gusted as high as 45 to 55 mph led to the very rapid development of significant snow drifts and widespread blowing snow with near-blizzard conditions, on top of the added hazard of a flash freeze.||Wind diminished after 6 pm March 16, but strengthened again during the afternoon of March 17, with very brief visibility of a quarter mile or less at many exposed locations. I-90 west of Sioux Falls and I-29 north of Sioux Falls were closed, along with no travel recommendations on secondary roads, into early Saturday morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief period of light freezing rain resulted in a light glaze of icing, prior to a period of snowfall that accumulated to 1.2 inches at COOP site MRFS2 in Madison. Northerly wind increased by the afternoon of March 16 with gusts as high as 44 mph at Automated Weather Observing System site KMDS at Madison, resulting in periodic visibility from one-half to one mile. After winds decreased overnight and early morning of March 17 with minimal impacts, winds increased again during the later morning and afternoon with frequent convective snow showers that briefly reduced visibility at times to one-half mile or less.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system developed east of the Rockies in Colorado and then moved through the Great Plains and into the Upper Mississippi Valley from the evening of March 30th through the evening of March 31st. This system produced heavy snow across parts of central and northeastern South Dakota, along with wind gusts of 40 to 55 mph. Some of the heaviest amounts include 18 inches 5 miles north-northwest of Miranda, 16 inches 7 miles east of Hayes, 15 inches 8 miles south-southwest of Highmore, and 14 inches at Wilmot, Faulkton and a mile northwest of Pierre. The result was blizzard conditions over much of central and northeastern South Dakota during the day and evening on March 31st. In addition, this system produced thunderstorms with small hail, sleet, and thundersnow across the area, as well as ice accumulations of over a quarter of an inch across portions of northeastern South Dakota. ||Impacts were significant, including widespread school closures, the cancellation of the YMCA Interstate Basketball tournament, widespread impassable or closed roads including I-90 from Wall to Chamberlain beginning at noon on the 31st and I-29 from 2pm on March 31st to noon on April 1st from Watertown to the North Dakota border, a great many stranded vehicles and vehicle accidents, power outages that lasted overnight for many customers, and cattle and ranchers during peak calving season were adversely affected. Finally, the heavy snowfall in Aberdeen was found to have caused the collapse of two-thirds of the Van Slyke building in downtown. A fire also started, but was contained soon thereafter. No injuries were reported. This heavy snow event further set the stage for significant spring flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak low pressure system tracked from southeastern North Dakota from the evening of March 11th into northwestern Wisconsin by noon on March 12th. Meanwhile, high pressure built in from the west, leading to a tight pressure gradient and gusty northerly winds of 35 to 45 mph across northeastern South Dakota through the morning and afternoon on March 12th. Drifting and blowing of loose snow across roadways led to reduced visibility and icy conditions, as the snow melted and re-froze. Several vehicle accidents resulted across the area, including one that involved an injury in Hamlin County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A rollover accident occurred due to icy roads around 12:40 pm on March 12th, 6 miles northeast of Castlewood near County Road 465 and 183rd Street. The driver was taken to a hospital for injuries from the crash.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Light drizzle and freezing drizzle during the afternoon and evening of March 8th gradually mixed in with and transitioned to a light, long-duration snowfall through the day on March 9th in response to increasing 850 hPa warm air advection and low-level saturation. Snow slowly dissipated from west to east late in the evening on March 9th and into the early overnight on March 10th. Ice accumulations were a glaze at best given warmer ground temperatures and total snowfall ranged from 1 to 5 inches across the region. Winds were light through the event, resulting in minimal overall impact.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light drizzle or freezing drizzle slowly changed over to light snow, accumulating to 5.0 inches at the CoCoRaHS site 2.2 miles south-southeast of Astoria and 4.0 inches at the CoCoRaHS site 5.3 miles west of Bruce. In addition, the COOP observer 2 miles northeast of Brookings measured 2.6 inches of snow. Ice accumulation was minimal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A rapidly strengthening storm developed across the Northern Plains and into the western Great Lakes on March 16-17. An initial band of precipitation developed during the early morning hours of March 16 from eastern South Dakota into southwestern Minnesota. While precipitation started in many locations as light rain or brief light freezing rain, a sharp fall in temperatures in the morning hours forced a change to a couple hours of heavy snowfall, accompanied by increasing northerly wind. With most ditches already full and snow depths the morning of March 16 between 5 and 15 inches near and north of Interstate 90, the new snowfall and winds which gusted as high as 45 to 55 mph led to the very rapid development of significant snow drifts and widespread blowing snow with near-blizzard conditions, on top of the added hazard of a flash freeze.||Wind diminished after 6 pm March 16, but strengthened again during the afternoon of March 17, with very brief visibility of a quarter mile or less at many exposed locations. I-90 west of Sioux Falls and I-29 north of Sioux Falls were closed, along with no travel recommendations on secondary roads, into early Saturday morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief period of light freezing rain resulted in a light glaze of icing, prior to a period of snowfall that accumulated to 3.5 inches at the COOP site in Montrose, 3.1 inches at the COOP site near Salem, and 2.5 inches at the COOP site in Bridgewater. Northerly wind increased by the afternoon of March 16 with gusts as high as 44 mph at South Dakota Road Weather Information System site SD547 near Montrose, resulting in periodic visibility of one-half to one mile. After winds decreased overnight and early morning of March 17 with minimal impacts, winds increased again during the later morning and afternoon with frequent convective snow showers that briefly reduced visibility at times to one mile or less.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak area of low pressure produced high northwest winds across Lyman and Buffalo Counties during the early morning hours of March 1st, as it passed into northeastern South Dakota. The highest measured gusts include 61 mph 9 miles north-northwest of Vivian and 3 miles southeast of Reliance, and 58 mph 3 miles north of Gann Valley. Only minor snowfall amounts were observed in these areas with this system.

A broad area of low pressure generated a shield of steady light to moderate snow across central and northeastern South Dakota through the day on March 9th. A broad swath of 2 to 5 inches of snow was observed, with some enhancement from terrain on the eastern side of the Prairie Coteau. The highest measured amounts include 7.5 inches in Gary and a mile south of Pierre, 6.5 inches 2 miles south of Doland and in Labolt, 6.1 inches 2 miles west of Rauville, and 6 inches a mile north-northwest of Milbank.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A member of the public observed 7.5 inches of snow in Gary.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure tracked from southwestern Minnesota into far northwestern Wisconsin on March 16th and generated between a half and three inches of snow. Strong high pressure that followed resulted in a tight pressure gradient and strong northwest wind gusts of 40 to 55 mph that persisted between March 16th and 17th. Visibility was reduced from the blowing and drifting of both fresh and loose snow from the pre-existing deep snowpack, and hazardous travel was compounded when snow melted and then re-froze on road surfaces.   ||The South Dakota Department of Transportation issued several No Travel Advised due to hazardous or impassable roads from low visibility and significant drifting snow on both the 16th and 17th. Numerous vehicle accidents and/or stranded motorists were reported across the area. There were several temporary highway closures due to accidents on the 17th as well, including SD Highway 28 and SD Highway 22 in Hamlin County. I-29 was closed from Sioux Falls to the North Dakota border beginning at 6:00 pm on the 17th.

Finally, the heavy snowfall in Aberdeen was found to have caused the collapse of two-thirds of the Van Slyke building in downtown. A fire also started, but was contained soon thereafter. No injuries were reported. This heavy snow event further set the stage for significant spring flooding.

A strong low pressure system developed east of the Rockies in Colorado and then moved through the Great Plains and into the Upper Mississippi Valley from the evening of March 30th through the evening of March 31st. This system produced freezing rain during the late morning and afternoon on the 31st, with accumulations of a quarter inch or more across Deuel, Codington, Grant and Hamlin Counties. Some of the highest ice measurements include half an inch 3 miles south of Brandt and a third of an inch 4 miles south of Strandburg. Impacts from the ice included treacherous road conditions and localized power outages. In addition, this system produced wind gusts of 45 to 55 mph, thunderstorms with small hail, sleet, thundersnow, and blizzard conditions across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A quarter inch of ice accumulation was observed under the snow, which fell after the ice storm ended.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme (D3) drought conditions eased to Severe (D2) by mid-February.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Light drizzle and freezing drizzle during the afternoon and evening of March 8th gradually mixed in with and transitioned to a light, long-duration snowfall through the day on March 9th in response to increasing 850 hPa warm air advection and low-level saturation. Snow slowly dissipated from west to east late in the evening on March 9th and into the early overnight on March 10th. Ice accumulations were a glaze at best given warmer ground temperatures and total snowfall ranged from 1 to 5 inches across the region. Winds were light through the event, resulting in minimal overall impact.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light drizzle or freezing drizzle slowly changed over to light snow, accumulating to 1.1 inches at the COOP site in Tyndall. Ice accumulations were minimal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A rapidly strengthening storm developed across the Northern Plains and into the western Great Lakes on March 16-17. An initial band of precipitation developed during the early morning hours of March 16 from eastern South Dakota into southwestern Minnesota. While precipitation started in many locations as light rain or brief light freezing rain, a sharp fall in temperatures in the morning hours forced a change to a couple hours of heavy snowfall, accompanied by increasing northerly wind. With most ditches already full and snow depths the morning of March 16 between 5 and 15 inches near and north of Interstate 90, the new snowfall and winds which gusted as high as 45 to 55 mph led to the very rapid development of significant snow drifts and widespread blowing snow with near-blizzard conditions, on top of the added hazard of a flash freeze.||Wind diminished after 6 pm March 16, but strengthened again during the afternoon of March 17, with very brief visibility of a quarter mile or less at many exposed locations. I-90 west of Sioux Falls and I-29 north of Sioux Falls were closed, along with no travel recommendations on secondary roads, into early Saturday morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief period of light freezing rain resulted in a glaze of icing, prior to a period of snowfall that accumulated to 3.3 inches at the COOP site near Forestburg. Northerly wind increased by the afternoon of March 16 with gusts as high as 43 mph at South Dakota Road Weather Information System site SD527 near Forestburg, resulting in periodic visibility around one mile.  After winds decreased overnight and early morning of March 17 with minimal impacts, winds increased again during the later morning and afternoon with frequent convective snow showers that briefly reduced visibility at times to one-half mile or less.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure tracked from southwestern Minnesota into far northwestern Wisconsin on March 16th and generated between a half and three inches of snow. Strong high pressure that followed resulted in a tight pressure gradient and strong northwest wind gusts of 40 to 55 mph that persisted between March 16th and 17th. Visibility was reduced from the blowing and drifting of both fresh and loose snow from the pre-existing deep snowpack, and hazardous travel was compounded when snow melted and then re-froze on road surfaces.   ||The South Dakota Department of Transportation issued several No Travel Advised due to hazardous or impassable roads from low visibility and significant drifting snow on both the 16th and 17th. Numerous vehicle accidents and/or stranded motorists were reported across the area. There were several temporary highway closures due to accidents on the 17th as well, including SD Highway 28 and SD Highway 22 in Hamlin County. I-29 was closed from Sioux Falls to the North Dakota border beginning at 6:00 pm on the 17th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level short-wave and mid-level front produced areas of light to moderate snow across far southeast South Dakota, northwest Iowa, and northeast Nebraska during the late evening of March 22nd into the early overnight hours of March 23rd. Stronger frontal forcing resulted in snow accumulations from 2 to 6 inches. In addition, thundersnow and periods of graupel were observed at times. Winds were relatively weak with this system, such that overall impacts were minimal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The CoCoRaHS observer 0.3 miles east-northeast of Yankton measured 3.5 inches of snow and the COOP observer 2 miles east of Yankton measured 3.0 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long-duration winter storm developed across southeast South Dakota on February 21, with waves of heavier snowfall across the region as winds increased to a gusty 30 to 50 mph.  Blizzard conditions developed during the afternoon of February 22, with strong winds lingering into the midday hours of February 23.  During the event, widespread blowing and drifting snowfall led to the closure of Interstate 90, as well as Interstate 29 north of Sioux Falls.  Travel was not recommended in numerous counties, with schools and county/state offices closed due to the hazardous conditions. Total snowfall varied from around 3 inches in extreme southeast South Dakota to around 17 inches near Huron.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Over the two-day period, snowfall accumulated to 15.0 inches at Forestburg and 14.9 inches at Woonsocket.  Whiteout conditions became widespread, especially in rural areas, due to winds which peaked at 42 mph near Forestburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm briefly became severe over the Piedmont and Summerset areas, producing hail to golf ball size.
A thunderstorm quickly became severe in the Rapid City area and tracked eastward onto the adjacent plains. Hail around quarter size accompanied the storm.

A long-lived supercell thunderstorm developed over the northern Black Hills and tracked eastward across west central South Dakota, producing large hail and wind gusts over 70 mph. Considerable damage to property was reported north of Rapid City as the storm passed through the area.

Strong low pressure moved from the central Plains early morning on March 31 into the western Great Lakes by early morning April 1. The area was just on the south side of the more significant banded precipitation, and featured enough instability for a little thunder to mix in during the morning hours.  Most precipitation started as rain or freezing rain, mixing with and changing to snow through the afternoon and early evening from west to east. Winds behind the system increased to a gusty 30 to 40 mph at the peak, with snowfall generally from 2 to 4 inches north of I-90 and west of the James River, as well as southwest Minnesota mainly north of Highway 14.  The combination of wind and snowfall led to some near-blizzard conditions for a brief time during the late evening and early overnight hours of April 1.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light mixed precipitation produced a glaze of icing during the afternoon, with a change to snow during the evening. Total snow accumulation reached 2.0 inches at a CoCoRaHS site near Astoria and 1.0 inch near White. Northerly winds reached gusts as high as 38 mph at Automated Weather Observing System site KBKX in Brookings, with visibilities briefly one mile or less between 2130-2400CST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Light drizzle and freezing drizzle during the afternoon and evening of March 8th gradually mixed in with and transitioned to a light, long-duration snowfall through the day on March 9th in response to increasing 850 hPa warm air advection and low-level saturation. Snow slowly dissipated from west to east late in the evening on March 9th and into the early overnight on March 10th. Ice accumulations were a glaze at best given warmer ground temperatures and total snowfall ranged from 1 to 5 inches across the region. Winds were light through the event, resulting in minimal overall impact.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light drizzle or freezing drizzle slowly changed over to light snow, accumulating to 2.2 inches at the NWS office in Sioux Falls and 2.7 inches at the COOP site in Colton. In addition, the CoCoRaHS observer 5.2 miles east of Sioux Falls measured 2.6 inches and the CoCoRaHS observer 6.4 miles south of Hartford measured 2.5 inches of snow. Ice accumulation was minimal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Northwest winds from 10 to 20 mph with temperatures falling to the teens below zero produced wind chills from 30 below to 40 below zero.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chills dropped as low as -35 4 miles west of Plankinton, but for much less than an hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Northwest winds from 10 to 20 mph with temperatures falling to the teens below zero produced wind chills from 30 below to 40 below zero.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chills dropped as low as -30 5 miles south of Arlington.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A rapidly strengthening storm developed across the Northern Plains and into the western Great Lakes on March 16-17. An initial band of precipitation developed during the early morning hours of March 16 from eastern South Dakota into southwestern Minnesota. While precipitation started in many locations as light rain or brief light freezing rain, a sharp fall in temperatures in the morning hours forced a change to a couple hours of heavy snowfall, accompanied by increasing northerly wind. With most ditches already full and snow depths the morning of March 16 between 5 and 15 inches near and north of Interstate 90, the new snowfall and winds which gusted as high as 45 to 55 mph led to the very rapid development of significant snow drifts and widespread blowing snow with near-blizzard conditions, on top of the added hazard of a flash freeze.||Wind diminished after 6 pm March 16, but strengthened again during the afternoon of March 17, with very brief visibility of a quarter mile or less at many exposed locations. I-90 west of Sioux Falls and I-29 north of Sioux Falls were closed, along with no travel recommendations on secondary roads, into early Saturday morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall accumulated to 4.0 inches at the CoCoRaHS site at Wagner and 3.0 inches at the COOP site at Platte. Northerly wind increased by the afternoon of March 16 with gusts as high as 44 mph at South Dakota Road Weather Information System site SD554 13 miles west of Platte, resulting in periodic visibility from one-half to one mile. After winds decreased overnight and early morning of March 17 with minimal impacts, winds increased again during the later morning and afternoon with a few convective snow showers that briefly reduced visibility at times to one mile or less.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system developed east of the Rockies in Colorado and then moved through the Great Plains and into the Upper Mississippi Valley from the evening of March 30th through the evening of March 31st. This system produced heavy snow across parts of central and northeastern South Dakota, along with wind gusts of 40 to 55 mph. Some of the heaviest amounts include 18 inches 5 miles north-northwest of Miranda, 16 inches 7 miles east of Hayes, 15 inches 8 miles south-southwest of Highmore, and 14 inches at Wilmot, Faulkton and a mile northwest of Pierre. The result was blizzard conditions over much of central and northeastern South Dakota during the day and evening on March 31st. In addition, this system produced thunderstorms with small hail, sleet, and thundersnow across the area, as well as ice accumulations of over a quarter of an inch across portions of northeastern South Dakota. ||Impacts were significant, including widespread school closures, the cancellation of the YMCA Interstate Basketball tournament, widespread impassable or closed roads including I-90 from Wall to Chamberlain beginning at noon on the 31st and I-29 from 2pm on March 31st to noon on April 1st from Watertown to the North Dakota border, a great many stranded vehicles and vehicle accidents, power outages that lasted overnight for many customers, and cattle and ranchers during peak calving season were adversely affected. Finally, the heavy snowfall in Aberdeen was found to have caused the collapse of two-thirds of the Van Slyke building in downtown. A fire also started, but was contained soon thereafter. No injuries were reported. This heavy snow event further set the stage for significant spring flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blizzard conditions were reported in Miller, and a COOP observer there measured 6 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long-duration winter storm developed across southeast South Dakota on February 21, with waves of heavier snowfall across the region as winds increased to a gusty 30 to 50 mph.  Blizzard conditions developed during the afternoon of February 22, with strong winds lingering into the midday hours of February 23.  During the event, widespread blowing and drifting snowfall led to the closure of Interstate 90, as well as Interstate 29 north of Sioux Falls.  Travel was not recommended in numerous counties, with schools and county/state offices closed due to the hazardous conditions. Total snowfall varied from around 3 inches in extreme southeast South Dakota to around 17 inches near Huron.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Over the two-day period, snowfall accumulated to 13.0 inches at De Smet and 11.5 inches at Iroquois.  Whiteout conditions became widespread, especially in rural areas, due to winds which peaked at 42 mph near Arlington.

The combination of wind and snowfall led to some near-blizzard conditions for a brief time during the late evening and early overnight hours of April 1.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow was briefly moderate during the late morning into early afternoon, with total accumulation of 3.6 inches at COOP site CBRS2 5 miles south of Chamberlain, 2.7 inches at a CoCoRaHS site in Chamberlain, and 2.0 inches near Pukwana.  Northerly winds reached gusts as high as 42 mph at South Dakota Road Weather Information System site SD537 at Kimball and 38 mph at Automated Weather Observing System site K9V9 in Chamberlain, with visibilities briefly one mile or less between 1800-2200CST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A rapidly strengthening storm developed across the Northern Plains and into the western Great Lakes on March 16-17. An initial band of precipitation developed during the early morning hours of March 16 from eastern South Dakota into southwestern Minnesota. While precipitation started in many locations as light rain or brief light freezing rain, a sharp fall in temperatures in the morning hours forced a change to a couple hours of heavy snowfall, accompanied by increasing northerly wind. With most ditches already full and snow depths the morning of March 16 between 5 and 15 inches near and north of Interstate 90, the new snowfall and winds which gusted as high as 45 to 55 mph led to the very rapid development of significant snow drifts and widespread blowing snow with near-blizzard conditions, on top of the added hazard of a flash freeze.||Wind diminished after 6 pm March 16, but strengthened again during the afternoon of March 17, with very brief visibility of a quarter mile or less at many exposed locations. I-90 west of Sioux Falls and I-29 north of Sioux Falls were closed, along with no travel recommendations on secondary roads, into early Saturday morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief period of light freezing rain resulted in a light glaze of icing, prior to a period of snowfall that accumulated to 1.0 inches at a CoCoRaHS site 5 miles west of Howard. Northerly wind increased by the afternoon of March 16 with gusts as high as 38 mph at South Dakota Road Weather Information System site SD597 near Howard, resulting in periodic visibility from one-half to one mile. After winds decreased overnight and early morning of March 17 with minimal impacts, winds increased again during the later morning and afternoon with frequent convective snow showers that briefly reduced visibility at times to one-half mile or less.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system developed east of the Rockies in Colorado and then moved through the Great Plains and into the Upper Mississippi Valley from the evening of March 30th through the evening of March 31st. This system produced heavy snow across parts of central and northeastern South Dakota, along with wind gusts of 40 to 55 mph. Some of the heaviest amounts include 18 inches 5 miles north-northwest of Miranda, 16 inches 7 miles east of Hayes, 15 inches 8 miles south-southwest of Highmore, and 14 inches at Wilmot, Faulkton and a mile northwest of Pierre. The result was blizzard conditions over much of central and northeastern South Dakota during the day and evening on March 31st. In addition, this system produced thunderstorms with small hail, sleet, and thundersnow across the area, as well as ice accumulations of over a quarter of an inch across portions of northeastern South Dakota. ||Impacts were significant, including widespread school closures, the cancellation of the YMCA Interstate Basketball tournament, widespread impassable or closed roads including I-90 from Wall to Chamberlain beginning at noon on the 31st and I-29 from 2pm on March 31st to noon on April 1st from Watertown to the North Dakota border, a great many stranded vehicles and vehicle accidents, power outages that lasted overnight for many customers, and cattle and ranchers during peak calving season were adversely affected. Finally, the heavy snowfall in Aberdeen was found to have caused the collapse of two-thirds of the Van Slyke building in downtown. A fire also started, but was contained soon thereafter. No injuries were reported. This heavy snow event further set the stage for significant spring flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The weather station at the Aberdeen Regional Airport reported blizzard conditions. The official National Weather Service observation there indicated 7.5 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long-duration winter storm developed across southeast South Dakota on February 21, with waves of heavier snowfall across the region as winds increased to a gusty 30 to 50 mph.  Blizzard conditions developed during the afternoon of February 22, with strong winds lingering into the midday hours of February 23.  During the event, widespread blowing and drifting snowfall led to the closure of Interstate 90, as well as Interstate 29 north of Sioux Falls.  Travel was not recommended in numerous counties, with schools and county/state offices closed due to the hazardous conditions. Total snowfall varied from around 3 inches in extreme southeast South Dakota to around 17 inches near Huron.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Over the two-day period, snowfall accumulated to 13.0 inches at Wessington Springs.  Whiteout conditions became widespread, especially in rural areas, due to winds which peaked at 42 mph near Lane.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms moved across portions of southeast South Dakota during the evening of March 30th and early morning hours of March 31st. A couple of the strongest storms produced severe hail soon after crossing the Missouri River.

Strong low pressure moved from the central Plains early morning on March 31 into the western Great Lakes by early morning April 1. The area was just on the south side of the more significant banded precipitation, and featured enough instability for a little thunder to mix in during the morning hours.  Most precipitation started as rain or freezing rain, mixing with and changing to snow through the afternoon and early evening from west to east. Winds behind the system increased to a gusty 30 to 40 mph at the peak, with snowfall generally from 2 to 4 inches north of I-90 and west of the James River, as well as southwest Minnesota mainly north of Highway 14.  The combination of wind and snowfall led to some near-blizzard conditions for a brief time during the late evening and early overnight hours of April 1.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light mixed precipitation produced a glaze of icing during the afternoon, with a change to snow during the evening. Total snow accumulation reached 6.0 inches at COOP site WTNS2 2 miles southeast of Wessington and 2.0 inches at Huron.  Northerly winds reached gusts as high as 51 mph at Automated Surface Observing System site KHON in Huron, and 34 mph at South Dakota Road Weather Information System site SD515 near Cavour, with visibilities briefly one mile or less between 2000-2300CST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Light drizzle and freezing drizzle during the afternoon and evening of March 8th gradually mixed in with and transitioned to a light, long-duration snowfall through the day on March 9th in response to increasing 850 hPa warm air advection and low-level saturation. Snow slowly dissipated from west to east late in the evening on March 9th and into the early overnight on March 10th. Ice accumulations were a glaze at best given warmer ground temperatures and total snowfall ranged from 1 to 5 inches across the region. Winds were light through the event, resulting in minimal overall impact.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light drizzle or freezing drizzle slowly changed over to light snow, accumulating to 5.0 inches at the COOP site in Wessington Springs. Ice accumulations were minimal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long-lived severe thunderstorm tracked from Wyoming across portions of the northern Black Hills area and southern Butte County, producing large hail and gusty winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mostly marble sized hail with a few quarters.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Light drizzle and freezing drizzle during the afternoon and evening of March 8th gradually mixed in with and transitioned to a light, long-duration snowfall through the day on March 9th in response to increasing 850 hPa warm air advection and low-level saturation. Snow slowly dissipated from west to east late in the evening on March 9th and into the early overnight on March 10th. Ice accumulations were a glaze at best given warmer ground temperatures and total snowfall ranged from 1 to 5 inches across the region. Winds were light through the event, resulting in minimal overall impact.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light drizzle or freezing drizzle slowly changed over to light snow, accumulating to 6.0 inches at the COOP site in De Smet. In addition, the COOP site in Iroquois measured 5.0 inches and the CoCoRaHS site 9 miles south-southwest of Arlington measured 4.5 inches of snow. Ice accumulations were minimal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level short-wave and mid-level front produced areas of light to moderate snow across far southeast South Dakota, northwest Iowa, and northeast Nebraska during the late evening of March 22nd into the early overnight hours of March 23rd. Stronger frontal forcing resulted in snow accumulations from 2 to 6 inches. In addition, thundersnow and periods of graupel were observed at times. Winds were relatively weak with this system, such that overall impacts were minimal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The COOP observer in Tyndall measured 1.0 inch of snow.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The CoCoRaHS observer 1.4 miles southeast of Vermillion measured 5.0 inches of snow, the CoCoRaHS observer 2.3 miles northwest of Ponca measured 2.3 inches of snow, and the COOP observer 6 miles southeast of Centerville measured 2.0 inches of snow. There was also a public report of 3.0 inches of snow 5 miles north of Elk Point.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Northwest winds from 10 to 20 mph with temperatures falling to the teens below zero produced wind chills from 30 below to 40 below zero.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chills dropped as low as -33 at Brookings.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chills dropped as low as -34 4 miles east-northeast of Alexandria.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system developed east of the Rockies in Colorado and then moved through the Great Plains and into the Upper Mississippi Valley from the evening of March 30th through the evening of March 31st. This system produced heavy snow across parts of central and northeastern South Dakota, along with wind gusts of 40 to 55 mph. Some of the heaviest amounts include 18 inches 5 miles north-northwest of Miranda, 16 inches 7 miles east of Hayes, 15 inches 8 miles south-southwest of Highmore, and 14 inches at Wilmot, Faulkton and a mile northwest of Pierre. The result was blizzard conditions over much of central and northeastern South Dakota during the day and evening on March 31st. In addition, this system produced thunderstorms with small hail, sleet, and thundersnow across the area, as well as ice accumulations of over a quarter of an inch across portions of northeastern South Dakota. ||Impacts were significant, including widespread school closures, the cancellation of the YMCA Interstate Basketball tournament, widespread impassable or closed roads including I-90 from Wall to Chamberlain beginning at noon on the 31st and I-29 from 2pm on March 31st to noon on April 1st from Watertown to the North Dakota border, a great many stranded vehicles and vehicle accidents, power outages that lasted overnight for many customers, and cattle and ranchers during peak calving season were adversely affected. Finally, the heavy snowfall in Aberdeen was found to have caused the collapse of two-thirds of the Van Slyke building in downtown. A fire also started, but was contained soon thereafter. No injuries were reported. This heavy snow event further set the stage for significant spring flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blizzard conditions were reported in Murdo, and a COOP observer there measured 9.7 inches of snow.

Up to 200 percent of normal precipitation during February resulted in a small improvement in many locations to drought conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme (D3) drought conditions eased to Severe (D2) by mid-February.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak area of low pressure across southwestern South Dakota helped promote the development of snow and pockets of heavy banded snow from the morning of March 5th through the morning of March 6th. Generally 2 to 6 inches of snow was observed, with localized higher amounts. Hazardous travel conditions due to snow and ice-covered roadways resulted in several vehicle accidents, including one that led to a serious injury in Hughes County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A one-vehicle rollover crash east of Pierre on Highway 14 around 7:30 am on March 6th resulted in serious, non-life threatening injuries. The driver lost control due to slush on the road surface.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak low pressure system tracked from southeastern North Dakota from the evening of March 11th into northwestern Wisconsin by noon on March 12th. Meanwhile, high pressure built in from the west, leading to a tight pressure gradient and gusty northerly winds of 35 to 45 mph across northeastern South Dakota through the morning and afternoon on March 12th. Drifting and blowing of loose snow across roadways led to reduced visibility and icy conditions, as the snow melted and re-froze. Several vehicle accidents resulted across the area, including one that involved an injury in Hamlin County.

A strong spring storm tracked across the region, producing heavy snow and blizzard conditions at times across portions of western South Dakota. The heaviest snow developed across southwestern South Dakota, where a foot to over two feet of snow was reported; the highest amounts were over the eastern slopes of the central and southern Black Hills. Elsewhere to the north and east, amounts tapered off, with six to 12 inches in many other areas and only a few inches across far northwestern and far south central South Dakota. Some freezing rain and mixed precipitation developed across south central South Dakota as well. Winds gusting around 40 mph on the South Dakota plains produced blizzard or near blizzard conditions at times, along with significant drifting snow.

A line of thunderstorms produced a few gusts around 60 mph over portions of Fall River and Custer Counties.

Temperatures averaged 3 to 5 degrees above normal during September, with precipitation varying between 25 to 75 percent of normal east of the James River to around normal in south central South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) drought conditions persisted through September.

A strong low pressure system developed across the western Great Plains and strengthened as it tracked northeast into Iowa on the afternoon of April 4th and into northeastern Minnesota by noon on April 5th. This system produced accumulating snow as well as a wintry mix across central and northeastern South Dakota on April 4th, and then strong west-northwest wind gusts between 30 and 45 mph on April 5th. The result was significantly reduced visibilities at times. Ditches were full of snow from this and previous snowfall events, which allowed for significant drifts to develop on roadways as well.||Many US and state highways were either impassable, closed or under no travel advised statements from the South Dakota Department of Transportation. Interstate 29 closed from Watertown to the North Dakota boarder from 2pm April 4th through 7am April 6th. Business and school closures were widespread as a result. Additionally, there were several reports of power outages, and ranchers were adversely impacted due to the storm's overlap with the calving season. Following this storm, many locations had observed a top-10 winter season for total snowfall. All the snow and much below average temperatures to start April set the stage for significant spring flooding.

Just 4 days after a major winter storm at the end of March, a strong low pressure system developed across the western Great Plains and strengthened as it tracked northeast into Iowa on the afternoon of April 4th and into northeastern Minnesota by noon on April 5th. This system produced heavy snow from late in the evening on April 3rd through early in the morning on April 5th across central and northeastern South Dakota, along with northeast winds of 15-30mph. The initial snow over east central South Dakota transitioned to a freezing rain or wintry mix for a period of time during the late afternoon and evening hours before switching back to snow. Visibilities were frequently reduced to between a mile and a quarter mile in heavy snow, with localized blizzard conditions at times. Winds shifted to the west-northwest on April 5th and gusted to between 30 and 45 mph, which led to continued significantly reduced visibilities at times through the day despite the lack of new falling snow. Ditches were full of snow from this and previous snowfall events, which allowed for significant drifts to develop on roadways. Some of the highest snowfall totals included 18 inches 7 miles east of Hayes, 16 inches 8 miles south-southwest of Seneca, 15.6 inches a mile west of Roscoe, 15 inches 9 miles east of Grey Goose, 4 miles northwest of Onida and 11 miles northeast of Pierre, and 14 inches in Bowdle, 5 miles northwest of Mission Ridge and 3 miles northwest of Westport.||Many US and state highways were either impassable, closed or under no travel advised statements from the South Dakota Department of Transportation. Interstate 29 closed from Watertown to the North Dakota boarder from 2pm April 4th through 7am April 6th. Business and school closures were widespread as a result. Governor Kristi Noem ordered the closure of state government executive branch offices from April 4th through 5th for numerous counties. Additionally, there were several reports of power outages, and ranchers were severely impacted due to the storm's overlap with the calving season. Finally, the roof of a downtown Aberdeen building, Dacotah Bank, began to collapse on the evening of April 6th due to the weight of the heavy snow. Following this storm, many locations had observed a top-10 winter season for total snowfall. April snow depth records were shattered at Aberdeen and Sisseton as well. All the snow and much below average temperatures to start April set the stage for significant spring flooding.
