All the snow and much below average temperatures to start April set the stage for significant spring flooding.

A strong low pressure system developed across the western Great Plains and strengthened as it tracked northeast into Iowa on the afternoon of April 4th and into northeastern Minnesota by noon on April 5th. This system produced accumulating snow as well as a wintry mix across central and northeastern South Dakota on April 4th, and then strong west-northwest wind gusts between 30 and 45 mph on April 5th. The result was significantly reduced visibilities at times. Ditches were full of snow from this and previous snowfall events, which allowed for significant drifts to develop on roadways as well.||Many US and state highways were either impassable, closed or under no travel advised statements from the South Dakota Department of Transportation. Interstate 29 closed from Watertown to the North Dakota boarder from 2pm April 4th through 7am April 6th. Business and school closures were widespread as a result. Additionally, there were several reports of power outages, and ranchers were adversely impacted due to the storm's overlap with the calving season. Following this storm, many locations had observed a top-10 winter season for total snowfall. All the snow and much below average temperatures to start April set the stage for significant spring flooding.

Scattered thunderstorms developed during peak heating of the afternoon in temperatures around or above 90 degrees, and intensified as they drifted west-northwest under the influence of a westward-moving high pressure center aloft. Strong to occasionally severe downdraft wind gusts resulted across portions of northeast and north central South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind tipped a trailer on US Highway 281, 2 miles south of Aberdeen.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snowmelt resulted in flooding along the James River and its associated tributaries. The National Weather Service recorded an April record snow depth of 26 inches in Aberdeen on the 5th. This was followed by near normal temperatures through the latter half of the month of April. This relatively mild warmup resulted in snowmelt on top of frozen ground, and forced most of the melt into the waterways. River gauges near Columbia peaked just below major flood stage at 17.85 feet on April 30th and near Stratford just below moderate flood stage with 16.75 feet on April 30th. The gauge near Ashton peaked well above major flood stage at 19.91 feet on April 21st, which overtopped one of the bridges and impacted another near the gauge site. The gauge near Redfield peaked at 22.3 feet on April 22nd, just above moderate flood stage. Aside from the bridges and a few roads that were impacted, the main result of this high water was flooding of agricultural lands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The James River near Stratford gauge peaked just below moderate flood stage with 16.75 feet on April 30th. This flooded significant amounts of agricultural lands as well as portions of 137th Street and 140th Street.

A snow squall developed along a cold front and moved southeast across parts of northeast South Dakota. The brief moderate to heavy snow and winds gusting to 35 to 55 mph brought temporary whiteout conditions to locations in Day County and the northwest part of Grant County. In southern Roberts County, accidents occurred along Interstate 29, with the southbound lane near the Wilmot exit becoming impassable due to over 20 vehicles being stopped or stuck. A rollover crash also occurred in the same area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement advised no travel in Roberts County due to multiple crashes and icy roadways.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail was mostly pea to dime size, with a few stones as large as a nickel.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Big Sioux River near Castlewood remained in flood at the start of May but by the early morning hours of May 5th, river levels had fallen below the flood stage of 9 feet.
Lake levels at Pelican Lake remained in flood from April through the start of May, with the lake level falling below the flood stage of 1713 feet on the evening of May 4th.

A line of storms developed in northeast South Dakota on the early afternoon of May 30th.  The strongest of the storms produced a wind gust of 60 mph at the South Dakota Department of Transportation Mesonet station 4 miles north of Altamont. Additionally, a trained spotter 6 miles north of Gary reported that the combination of wind and hail damaged beans crops and the plants in his garden.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The South Dakota Department of Transportation Mesonet station 4 miles north of Altamont recorded a wind gust of 60 mph.

A line of storms developed in the late afternoon and evening in western South Dakota. As they proceeded east, this line of storms produced an estimated 60 mph wind gust 3 miles west of Okaton before weakening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter estimated wind gusts associated with a thunderstorm were on the order of 50 to 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Wildfires in northwest Canada produced copious amounts of smoke which followed a cold front down into the Dakotas on May 17th and 18th. The visibility at Mobridge fell to 1/4 mile for an hour and a half on the afternoon of the 17th, and was impacted to some extent for a total of 21 hours. The visibility at Pierre was reduced for a total of 24 hour with a visibility down to a half mile. The visibility in Aberdeen fell to 3/4 of a mile and had visibility reductions for over 20 hours. Watertown had reductions down to a mile and overall visibility was reduced for a total of 18 hours. Measured daily Air Quality Index values ranged between 199 in Pierre, 162 at Aberdeen and 160 in Watertown on May 18th.

Just 4 days after a major winter storm at the end of March, a strong low pressure system developed across the western Great Plains and strengthened as it tracked northeast into Iowa on the afternoon of April 4th and into northeastern Minnesota by noon on April 5th. This system produced heavy snow from late in the evening on April 3rd through early in the morning on April 5th across central and northeastern South Dakota, along with northeast winds of 15-30mph. The initial snow over east central South Dakota transitioned to a freezing rain or wintry mix for a period of time during the late afternoon and evening hours before switching back to snow. Visibilities were frequently reduced to between a mile and a quarter mile in heavy snow, with localized blizzard conditions at times. Winds shifted to the west-northwest on April 5th and gusted to between 30 and 45 mph, which led to continued significantly reduced visibilities at times through the day despite the lack of new falling snow. Ditches were full of snow from this and previous snowfall events, which allowed for significant drifts to develop on roadways. Some of the highest snowfall totals included 18 inches 7 miles east of Hayes, 16 inches 8 miles south-southwest of Seneca, 15.6 inches a mile west of Roscoe, 15 inches 9 miles east of Grey Goose, 4 miles northwest of Onida and 11 miles northeast of Pierre, and 14 inches in Bowdle, 5 miles northwest of Mission Ridge and 3 miles northwest of Westport.||Many US and state highways were either impassable, closed or under no travel advised statements from the South Dakota Department of Transportation. Interstate 29 closed from Watertown to the North Dakota boarder from 2pm April 4th through 7am April 6th. Business and school closures were widespread as a result. Governor Kristi Noem ordered the closure of state government executive branch offices from April 4th through 5th for numerous counties. Additionally, there were several reports of power outages, and ranchers were severely impacted due to the storm's overlap with the calving season. Finally, the roof of a downtown Aberdeen building, Dacotah Bank, began to collapse on the evening of April 6th due to the weight of the heavy snow. Following this storm, many locations had observed a top-10 winter season for total snowfall. April snow depth records were shattered at Aberdeen and Sisseton as well. All the snow and much below average temperatures to start April set the stage for significant spring flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few compact thunderstorms developed along a warm front in eastern South Dakota. These low topped supercells tracked from just south of the Watertown area, towards Milbank and then on into western Minnesota between 430 and 630pm CDT before weakening. Several storm chasers confirmed two tornado touchdowns with the strongest of these storms. These weak tornadoes passed through mostly open fields.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm spotter posted photos of a rope tornado moving through pasture south of Strandburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Late afternoon thunderstorms developed in the exit region of a strong jet streak in northwest flow aloft, focused along a prefrontal trough across southeast South Dakota. Storms quickly became severe, with MLCAPE of up to 1500 J/kg and deep layer shear around 40 knots.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snowmelt resulted in flooding along the James River and its associated tributaries. The National Weather Service recorded an April record snow depth of 26 inches in Aberdeen on the 5th. This was followed by near normal temperatures through the latter half of the month of April. This relatively mild warmup resulted in snowmelt on top of frozen ground, and forced most of the melt into the waterways. River gauges near Columbia peaked just below major flood stage at 17.85 feet on April 30th and near Stratford just below moderate flood stage with 16.75 feet on April 30th. The gauge near Ashton peaked well above major flood stage at 19.91 feet on April 21st, which overtopped one of the bridges and impacted another near the gauge site. The gauge near Redfield peaked at 22.3 feet on April 22nd, just above moderate flood stage. Aside from the bridges and a few roads that were impacted, the main result of this high water was flooding of agricultural lands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowmelt resulted in flooding along Mud Creek. The river gauge near Brentford crested at 1276 feet on 4/17, a foot above flood stage. Water would have crossed 149th street at this height, although the main impact of this flooding would be on agricultural lands.

Ditches were full of snow from this and previous snowfall events, which allowed for significant drifts to develop on roadways. Some of the highest snowfall totals included 18 inches 7 miles east of Hayes, 16 inches 8 miles south-southwest of Seneca, 15.6 inches a mile west of Roscoe, 15 inches 9 miles east of Grey Goose, 4 miles northwest of Onida and 11 miles northeast of Pierre, and 14 inches in Bowdle, 5 miles northwest of Mission Ridge and 3 miles northwest of Westport.||Many US and state highways were either impassable, closed or under no travel advised statements from the South Dakota Department of Transportation. Interstate 29 closed from Watertown to the North Dakota boarder from 2pm April 4th through 7am April 6th. Business and school closures were widespread as a result. Governor Kristi Noem ordered the closure of state government executive branch offices from April 4th through 5th for numerous counties. Additionally, there were several reports of power outages, and ranchers were severely impacted due to the storm's overlap with the calving season. Finally, the roof of a downtown Aberdeen building, Dacotah Bank, began to collapse on the evening of April 6th due to the weight of the heavy snow. Following this storm, many locations had observed a top-10 winter season for total snowfall. April snow depth records were shattered at Aberdeen and Sisseton as well. All the snow and much below average temperatures to start April set the stage for significant spring flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm total 10 inches of snow was observed 11 miles southwest of Eureka.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few storms developed along a pre-frontal boundary in a zone of weak moisture convergence.  An abundance of dry air above the surface resulted in healthy downdrafts, which produced spotty thunderstorm wind gusts and some stronger non-thunderstorm winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts well away from thunderstorms were measured to 58 mph at a South Dakota Road Weather Information System site SD553 5 miles west of Plankinton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A snow squall developed along a cold front and moved southeast across parts of northeast South Dakota. The brief moderate to heavy snow and winds gusting to 35 to 55 mph brought temporary whiteout conditions to locations in Day County and the northwest part of Grant County. In southern Roberts County, accidents occurred along Interstate 29, with the southbound lane near the Wilmot exit becoming impassable due to over 20 vehicles being stopped or stuck. A rollover crash also occurred in the same area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The cooperative observer in Summit reported a snow squall with limited visibility.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snowmelt resulted in flooding along the James River and its associated tributaries. The National Weather Service recorded an April record snow depth of 26 inches in Aberdeen on the 5th. This was followed by near normal temperatures through the latter half of the month of April. This relatively mild warmup resulted in snowmelt on top of frozen ground, and forced most of the melt into the waterways. River gauges near Columbia peaked just below major flood stage at 17.85 feet on April 30th and near Stratford just below moderate flood stage with 16.75 feet on April 30th. The gauge near Ashton peaked well above major flood stage at 19.91 feet on April 21st, which overtopped one of the bridges and impacted another near the gauge site. The gauge near Redfield peaked at 22.3 feet on April 22nd, just above moderate flood stage. Aside from the bridges and a few roads that were impacted, the main result of this high water was flooding of agricultural lands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The James River near Columbia crested just below major flood stage at 17.85 feet on April 30th. This flooding impacted a significant amount of agricultural lands.

All the snow and much below average temperatures to start April set the stage for significant spring flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm total 8 inches of snow was observed in Miller.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Conditions averaged warm (3 to 5 degrees above normal) and dry (50-75 percent of normal) across southeast South Dakota during June and resulted in up to a slight worsening of drought conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate (D1) drought conditions worsened to severe (D2) during mid-June.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Despite less than 1000 J/kg MLCAPE and less than 30 knots deep-layer shear, a few storms managed to produce brief marginally large hail.
Despite less than 1000 J/kg MLCAPE and less than 30 knots deep-layer shear, a few storms managed to produce brief marginally large hail.

A weak wave produced a brief enhancement in mid-level warm advection, and with around 1000 J/kg elevated CAPE and marginal shear, a few storms developed with broad rotation and resulted in spotty large hail and damaging wind gusts.

A quick moving, but potent, storm system brought snowfall to much of western and south-central South Dakota. The heaviest snow developed across portions of the central Black Hills and adjacent plains during the late night and early morning hours, where amounts of eight to 15 inches were reported over many of those areas. Elsewhere, amounts of four to nine inches were common, with amounts falling off across far northwestern South Dakota. Wind gusts over 50 mph developed on the back side of the system over the southwestern and south central South Dakota plains, producing considerable blowing and drifting  snow. Interstate 90 was closed east of the Black Hills for a period due to near blizzard conditions at times.

The combination of a deep snowpack, a liquid equivalent exceeding 4 inches, and frozen soils due to persistently below-normal spring temperatures set the stage for flooding along the Big Sioux. However, a significant rise in temperatures, well above normal, triggered rapid snowmelt. The resulting runoff swiftly made its way into the Big Sioux River and its associated tributaries.||The Big Sioux river headwaters near Florence set a record flood stage. Points downstream along the Big Sioux River crested at moderate to major flood stage. Many local and county roads and bridges were impacted by the high water, along with widespread inundation of agricultural lands. ||A Presidential Disaster Declaration was approved for Brown, Clark, Codington, Day, Faulk, Grant, Hand, Marshall, Potter and Roberts Counties, as well as for the Lake Traverse Indian Reservation, where an estimated $2,305,362 in total qualifying costs were incurred during spring flooding. ||Apart from the mainstem river, there was heightened concern regarding Lake Kampeska. The Watertown Water Filtration plant and numerous local residences faced threats from both elevated lake water levels and the potential for ice breakup and shoves. In response, residents initiated sandbagging operations to safeguard their properties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pelican Lake levels to rose above flood stage on April 15th. The lake peaked at a level of 1713.6 on the afternoon of April 20th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The snowfall during the season in northeast and central South Dakota exceeded normal levels, with many locations ranking among the top 10 for the snowiest seasons. Additionally, the late spring conditions were unusually cold, resulting in a persistent and exceptionally deep snowpack until early April. The depth of the snowpack ranged from 15 to 30 plus inches, containing approximately 4 to 8 inches of snow water equivalent. However, the weather pattern shifted to abnormally warm conditions in the middle of April. Starting from April 9th, temperatures rapidly rose into the 40s and 50s in areas with deep snowpack, and occasionally reached the 60s and 70s thereafter. The rapid snowmelt that followed resulted in extensive overland flooding in the region between April 10th and 18th.||Flooding resulted in severe damage to public infrastructure. Several reports surfaced of county and township roads being washed out or submerged in water across central and northeastern South Dakota. The communities of Wilmot, SD, and Corona, SD, experienced significant and widespread flooding. In Grant County, SD, a water rescue was carried out after a motorist drove into a flooded roadway. Furthermore, flooding impacted infrastructure along Big Stone Lake in Grant County, Lake Kampeska in Codington County, and the Lake Traverse Reservation. A Presidential Disaster Declaration was approved for Brown, Clark, Codington, Day, Faulk, Grant, Hand, Marshall, Potter and Roberts Counties, as well as for the Lake Traverse Indian Reservation, where an estimated $2,305,362 in total qualifying costs were incurred.

During early April, the northeast part of South Dakota, including the Sisseton Hills region, experienced a considerable snow depth ranging from approximately 20 to 40 inches. On March 31st, Sisseton recorded a snow depth of 27 inches. Average temperatures for the month of March were 10 degrees below normal as well, with frozen soils across northeast South Dakota. In the second week of April, temperatures during the day warmed well above freezing resulting in rapid snowmelt from April 6th through April 12th. With a liquid water equivalent in excess of 4 inches, much of this melt entered straight into waterways resulting in flooding along the Little Minnesota River. The river near Peever crested at 22.53 feet, with water topping the bridges on the west side of Browns Valley and 1 mile north and 2 miles west of Browns Valley.||A Presidential Disaster Declaration was approved for Brown, Clark, Codington, Day, Faulk, Grant, Hand, Marshall, Potter and Roberts Counties, as well as for the Lake Traverse Indian Reservation, where an estimated $2,305,362 in total qualifying costs were incurred during spring flooding.

Much of the Elm River drainage basin experienced a cold slow start to spring, leaving the ground frozen when temperatures finally started to consistently warm above freezing during the second week in April. Snow water equivalent at the start of April was in excess of 4 inches, with most of the melt entering waterways and the resultant flooding mainly affecting agricultural lands.||A Presidential Disaster Declaration was approved for Brown, Clark, Codington, Day, Faulk, Grant, Hand, Marshall, Potter and Roberts Counties, as well as for the Lake Traverse Indian Reservation, where an estimated $2,305,362 in total qualifying costs were incurred during spring flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Elm River near Frederick crested at 14.27 in the early hours of April 15th, 1.27 feet above flood stage.

A boundary moving across southern Meade County produced a brief landspout tornado northeast of Ellsworth Air Force Base.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A landspout tornado formed over rangeland northeast of Ellsworth Air Force Base. It did not cause damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak area of low pressure tracked from west central South Dakota on the afternoon of February 28th to southwestern Minnesota by the late morning of March 1st. It produced periods of snow on the 28th and then a shield of snowfall along with more localized bands of heavier snows from north central and northeastern South Dakota into North Dakota and Minnesota through the morning of March 1st. Snowfall totals ranged from 2 to 9 inches. The highest reports include 9 inches 8 miles northwest of Leola and 2 miles south-southwest of McLaughlin, 8.1 inches 2 miles southeast of the Waubay National Wildlife Refuge, and 8 inches in Pollock. ||In addition to the snow, strong wind gusts of 40 to 55 mph led to significantly reduced visibility at times. The South Dakota Department of Transportation issued a No Travel Advisory across several counties as a result. Slick and snow-covered roads also contributed to several vehicle and semi truck accidents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nine inches of snow was observed 2 miles south-southwest of McLaughlin.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a consistently cold early spring with below-average temperatures and frozen soils, a mild period of weather emerged during the second week of April. The deep snow cover, exemplified by Milbank's report of a 17-inch snow depth on April 1st, along with a snow water equivalent exceeding 4 inches, rapidly transitioned into liquid form. This substantial snowmelt flowed into the waterways, leading to flooding along the Whetstone River. The South Fork Whetstone River gauge recorded a peak just above flood stage at 1113.83 feet, with water reaching the bottom of the bridge on 148th Street. On the evening of April 15th, the North Fork Whetstone River near Milbank gauge site reached a moderate flood stage of 1062.40 feet, resulting in water overflowing onto 479th Avenue and surpassing the bridge on 144th Street. Two other bridges were also affected as water levels rose. A significant portion of agricultural land was impacted. The Whetstone River gauge above Big Stone City registered a peak of 19.65 feet on April 13th, just below the threshold for a major flood stage.||A Presidential Disaster Declaration was approved for Brown, Clark, Codington, Day, Faulk, Grant, Hand, Marshall, Potter and Roberts Counties, as well as for the Lake Traverse Indian Reservation, where an estimated $2,305,362 in total qualifying costs were incurred during spring flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The South Fork Whetstone River gauge near Milbank peaked just above flood stage at 1113.83 feet, with water reaching the bottom of the bridge on 148th Street. Otherwise the impact was mainly to agricultural lands.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Light to moderate snowfall developed during the early morning hours of March 7th across Central South Dakota, and spread northeast through the rest of the calendar day. Steady accumulations totaled to between 2 and 6.5 inches by the morning of the 8th, with the highest totals including 6.5 inches in Bowdle, and 6 inches 8 miles south-southwest of Seneca and in Selby.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six and a half inches of snow was observed in Bowdle.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated pockets of thunderstorms developed along a stationary front across southeastern North Dakota during the late afternoon on June 7th, and then congealed as they drifted south into northeastern South Dakota. Two weather stations recorded severe-level wind gusts in Marshall and Roberts Counties, prior to the complex's dissipation during the early evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weather station from the South Dakota Department of Transportation measured a wind gust of 60 mph at Lake City.

Elsewhere, only a few inches fell.

A weak upper-level wave moving through southwest flow aloft, acting on an environment with 1000 to 1500 J/kg of MUCAPE in the vicinity of a surface trough, resulted in scattered thunderstorms across far southeast South Dakota, far northeast Nebraska, northwest Iowa, and southwest Minnesota during the evening of April 19th. The strongest storms produced severe hail ranging from 1.0 to 1.5 inches in diameter.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to 0.88 inch in diameter fell 10 miles east of Trent, covering the ground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The snowfall during the season in northeast and central South Dakota exceeded normal levels, with many locations ranking among the top 10 for the snowiest seasons. Additionally, the late spring conditions were unusually cold, resulting in a persistent and exceptionally deep snowpack until early April. The depth of the snowpack ranged from 15 to 30 plus inches, containing approximately 4 to 8 inches of snow water equivalent. However, the weather pattern shifted to abnormally warm conditions in the middle of April. Starting from April 9th, temperatures rapidly rose into the 40s and 50s in areas with deep snowpack, and occasionally reached the 60s and 70s thereafter. The rapid snowmelt that followed resulted in extensive overland flooding in the region between April 10th and 18th.||Flooding resulted in severe damage to public infrastructure. Several reports surfaced of county and township roads being washed out or submerged in water across central and northeastern South Dakota. The communities of Wilmot, SD, and Corona, SD, experienced significant and widespread flooding. In Grant County, SD, a water rescue was carried out after a motorist drove into a flooded roadway. Furthermore, flooding impacted infrastructure along Big Stone Lake in Grant County, Lake Kampeska in Codington County, and the Lake Traverse Reservation. A Presidential Disaster Declaration was approved for Brown, Clark, Codington, Day, Faulk, Grant, Hand, Marshall, Potter and Roberts Counties, as well as for the Lake Traverse Indian Reservation, where an estimated $2,305,362 in total qualifying costs were incurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system across far southwestern South Dakota on the early evening of June 23rd shifted northeast through the night, and reached far northeastern South Dakota by 10am on the 24th. Thunderstorms developed in the vicinity of this system as well as along a stationary front, and quickly became strong to severe. While initially discrete, cells eventually merged into a squall line. As a result, both large hail and damaging wind gusts locally in excess of 80 mph resulted across central South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A South Dakota Mesonet weather station recorded a wind gust of 63 mph.

An isolated thunderstorm developed, in the vicinity of a stationary front and low pressure system, across Perkins County and then tracked into Corson County during the early evening of June 21st. It produced a brief tornado in Perkins County, and a measured wind gust of 70 mph in Corson County before dissipating across far eastern Corson County near midnight CDT.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A personal weather station 6 miles south of Morristown recorded a wind gust of 70 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system across far southwestern South Dakota on the early evening of June 23rd shifted northeast through the night, and reached far northeastern South Dakota by 10am on the 24th. Thunderstorms developed in the vicinity of this system as well as along a stationary front, and quickly became strong to severe. While initially discrete, cells eventually merged into a squall line. As a result, both large hail and damaging wind gusts locally in excess of 80 mph resulted across central South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Estimated quarter to half dollar sized hail damaged crops. The timing of the hail was estimated by radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a consistently cold early spring with below-average temperatures and frozen soils, a mild period of weather emerged during the second week of April. The deep snow cover, exemplified by Milbank's report of a 17-inch snow depth on April 1st, along with a snow water equivalent exceeding 4 inches, rapidly transitioned into liquid form. This substantial snowmelt flowed into the waterways, leading to flooding along the Whetstone River. The South Fork Whetstone River gauge recorded a peak just above flood stage at 1113.83 feet, with water reaching the bottom of the bridge on 148th Street. On the evening of April 15th, the North Fork Whetstone River near Milbank gauge site reached a moderate flood stage of 1062.40 feet, resulting in water overflowing onto 479th Avenue and surpassing the bridge on 144th Street. Two other bridges were also affected as water levels rose. A significant portion of agricultural land was impacted. The Whetstone River gauge above Big Stone City registered a peak of 19.65 feet on April 13th, just below the threshold for a major flood stage.||A Presidential Disaster Declaration was approved for Brown, Clark, Codington, Day, Faulk, Grant, Hand, Marshall, Potter and Roberts Counties, as well as for the Lake Traverse Indian Reservation, where an estimated $2,305,362 in total qualifying costs were incurred during spring flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Whetstone River gauge above Big Stone City recorded a peak of 19.65 feet on April 13th, which is just below major flood stage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of a deep snowpack, a liquid equivalent exceeding 4 inches, and frozen soils due to persistently below-normal spring temperatures set the stage for flooding along the Big Sioux. However, a significant rise in temperatures, well above normal, triggered rapid snowmelt. The resulting runoff swiftly made its way into the Big Sioux River and its associated tributaries.||The Big Sioux river headwaters near Florence set a record flood stage. Points downstream along the Big Sioux River crested at moderate to major flood stage. Many local and county roads and bridges were impacted by the high water, along with widespread inundation of agricultural lands. ||A Presidential Disaster Declaration was approved for Brown, Clark, Codington, Day, Faulk, Grant, Hand, Marshall, Potter and Roberts Counties, as well as for the Lake Traverse Indian Reservation, where an estimated $2,305,362 in total qualifying costs were incurred during spring flooding. ||Apart from the mainstem river, there was heightened concern regarding Lake Kampeska. The Watertown Water Filtration plant and numerous local residences faced threats from both elevated lake water levels and the potential for ice breakup and shoves. In response, residents initiated sandbagging operations to safeguard their properties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Big Sioux river gauge in Watertown at Sioux Conifer Road peaked on the afternoon of April 12th at 10.9 which is moderate flood stage. This only affected pasture land in the area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) drought conditions improved to moderate (D1) conditions by mid-April.

The strong winds also produced areas of blowing snow and reduced visibility across portions of northwestern South Dakota where there was considerable snowpack.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The North Fork Whetstone River gauge near Milbank peaked in moderate flood stage at 1062.40 feet on the evening of April 15th, with water crossing over 479th Avenue and topping the bridge on 144th Street. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quick moving, but potent, storm system brought snowfall to much of western and south-central South Dakota. The heaviest snow developed across portions of the central Black Hills and adjacent plains during the late night and early morning hours, where amounts of eight to 15 inches were reported over many of those areas. Elsewhere, amounts of four to nine inches were common, with amounts falling off across far northwestern South Dakota. Wind gusts over 50 mph developed on the back side of the system over the southwestern and south central South Dakota plains, producing considerable blowing and drifting  snow. Interstate 90 was closed east of the Black Hills for a period due to near blizzard conditions at times.

Rapid snowmelt from a few day period of well above normal temperatures focused on the 11-13th of April resulted in minor to locally moderate flooding through much of the Big Sioux River Basin. Some areas north of I-90 lost between 2 to 5 inches of snow water equivalent (SWE) from the first to second week of April, but antecedent drought conditions resulted in only a percentage of this water actually making it into the river system.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Big Sioux River near Bruce, SD rose above flood stage on the morning of April 10th before cresting around 2.43 feet above flood stage (10.43 feet) on April 11th, which is in the Moderate category. The river then fell below flood stage on the morning of April 26th. The river was above Minor flood stage at this location for 16 days and above Moderate flood stage for 6 days.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rapid snowmelt from a few day period of well above normal temperatures focused on the 11-13th of April resulted in moderate to locally major flooding through much of the James River Basin. Some areas north of I-90 lost between 2 to 5 inches of snow water equivalent (SWE) from the first to second week of April, but antecedent drought conditions resulted in only a percentage of this water actually making it into the river system.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The James River near Mitchell, SD rose above flood stage during the early morning of April 18th before cresting around 4.03 feet above flood stage (21.03 feet) on April 28th, which is in the Moderate category. The river was above Minor flood stage at this location for 13 days and above Moderate stage for 6 days. Flooding continued into May.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The snowfall during the season in northeast and central South Dakota exceeded normal levels, with many locations ranking among the top 10 for the snowiest seasons. Additionally, the late spring conditions were unusually cold, resulting in a persistent and exceptionally deep snowpack until early April. The depth of the snowpack ranged from 15 to 30 plus inches, containing approximately 4 to 8 inches of snow water equivalent. However, the weather pattern shifted to abnormally warm conditions in the middle of April. Starting from April 9th, temperatures rapidly rose into the 40s and 50s in areas with deep snowpack, and occasionally reached the 60s and 70s thereafter. The rapid snowmelt that followed resulted in extensive overland flooding in the region between April 10th and 18th.||Flooding resulted in severe damage to public infrastructure. Several reports surfaced of county and township roads being washed out or submerged in water across central and northeastern South Dakota. The communities of Wilmot, SD, and Corona, SD, experienced significant and widespread flooding. In Grant County, SD, a water rescue was carried out after a motorist drove into a flooded roadway. Furthermore, flooding impacted infrastructure along Big Stone Lake in Grant County, Lake Kampeska in Codington County, and the Lake Traverse Reservation. A Presidential Disaster Declaration was approved for Brown, Clark, Codington, Day, Faulk, Grant, Hand, Marshall, Potter and Roberts Counties, as well as for the Lake Traverse Indian Reservation, where an estimated $2,305,362 in total qualifying costs were incurred.

A quick moving, but potent, storm system brought snowfall to much of western and south-central South Dakota. The heaviest snow developed across portions of the central Black Hills and adjacent plains during the late night and early morning hours, where amounts of eight to 15 inches were reported over many of those areas. Elsewhere, amounts of four to nine inches were common, with amounts falling off across far northwestern South Dakota. Wind gusts over 50 mph developed on the back side of the system over the southwestern and south central South Dakota plains, producing considerable blowing and drifting  snow. Interstate 90 was closed east of the Black Hills for a period due to near blizzard conditions at times.

A weak area of low pressure tracked from west central South Dakota on the afternoon of February 28th to southwestern Minnesota by the late morning of March 1st. It produced periods of snow on the 28th and then a shield of snowfall along with more localized bands of heavier snows from north central and northeastern South Dakota into North Dakota and Minnesota through the morning of March 1st. Snowfall totals ranged from 2 to 9 inches. The highest reports include 9 inches 8 miles northwest of Leola and 2 miles south-southwest of McLaughlin, 8.1 inches 2 miles southeast of the Waubay National Wildlife Refuge, and 8 inches in Pollock. ||In addition to the snow, strong wind gusts of 40 to 55 mph led to significantly reduced visibility at times. The South Dakota Department of Transportation issued a No Travel Advisory across several counties as a result. Slick and snow-covered roads also contributed to several vehicle and semi truck accidents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nine inches of snow was observed 8 miles northwest of Leola.

Much of the Elm River drainage basin experienced a cold slow start to spring, leaving the ground frozen when temperatures finally started to consistently warm above freezing during the second week in April. Snow water equivalent at the start of April was in excess of 4 inches, with most of the melt entering waterways and the resultant flooding mainly affecting agricultural lands.||A Presidential Disaster Declaration was approved for Brown, Clark, Codington, Day, Faulk, Grant, Hand, Marshall, Potter and Roberts Counties, as well as for the Lake Traverse Indian Reservation, where an estimated $2,305,362 in total qualifying costs were incurred during spring flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Elm River at Ordway crested at 14.78 feet, 1.78 feet above flood stage, on the evening of April 19th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The last of the seasonal snow cover melted off, but even with precipitation only at 75 percent or less of normal, near to slightly below average temperatures allowed most areas a slight improvement in drought conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) drought conditions improved to moderate (D1) conditions by mid-April.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of a deep snowpack, a liquid equivalent exceeding 4 inches, and frozen soils due to persistently below-normal spring temperatures set the stage for flooding along the Big Sioux. However, a significant rise in temperatures, well above normal, triggered rapid snowmelt. The resulting runoff swiftly made its way into the Big Sioux River and its associated tributaries.||The Big Sioux river headwaters near Florence set a record flood stage. Points downstream along the Big Sioux River crested at moderate to major flood stage. Many local and county roads and bridges were impacted by the high water, along with widespread inundation of agricultural lands. ||A Presidential Disaster Declaration was approved for Brown, Clark, Codington, Day, Faulk, Grant, Hand, Marshall, Potter and Roberts Counties, as well as for the Lake Traverse Indian Reservation, where an estimated $2,305,362 in total qualifying costs were incurred during spring flooding. ||Apart from the mainstem river, there was heightened concern regarding Lake Kampeska. The Watertown Water Filtration plant and numerous local residences faced threats from both elevated lake water levels and the potential for ice breakup and shoves. In response, residents initiated sandbagging operations to safeguard their properties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Big Sioux River gauge at Lake Kampeska peaked just below moderate flood stage at 21.93 feet.

Incoming low pressure from the west and high pressure to the east resulted in gusty south-southeasterly winds across northeastern South Dakota on the morning of March 14th. Pre-existing loose snow blew and drifted across roadways, and icy roads led to a fatal vehicle accident in Marshall County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A two-vehicle head-on crash occurred on the morning of March 14th east of Britton on South Dakota Highway 10 due to poor road conditions from winter weather. An 83-year-old man was killed, and a 19-year-old male sustained life-threatening injuries. South Dakota Highway 10 was closed to traffic for about 5 hours.

However, above average liquid equivalent precipitation of up to over half an inch (observed 0.75 to 1.5 plus inches) from March 1st through March 28th resulted in an improvement to D1 Abnormally Dry conditions in these areas.

An area of low pressure developed across south central South Dakota through the day on March 21st, and then tracked northeast into northern Minnesota by the morning of March 22nd. As it did so, this system produced a shield of snow across central and northeastern South Dakota, with moderate to heavy snowfall rates at times. Storm total snowfall by the evening of the 21st generally ranged between 2 and 5 inches across the area, with locally higher amounts including 6.2 inches 4 miles west of Sisseton and 6 inches 8 miles south-southwest of Keldron.

While the area began with next to no drought to start June, an extended period of significantly above normal temperatures by 8 to 13 degrees with highs in the 80s and 90s along with only spotty precipitation over the first third of the month resulted in the development and expansion of D2/Severe Drought in part across portions of south central South Dakota.||Regarding impacts, fine fuels quickly dried out which increased fire danger on dry and windy days. Winter wheat production was expected to be limited across areas that had missed out on localized rains through June 18th as well.

Several rounds of showers and thunderstorms across eastern Corson County from the afternoon of June 20th through the morning of the 23rd culminated with a widespread heavy rainfall event from the evening of the 23rd through the evening of the 24th. Some 5-day rainfall totals include 4.92 2 miles southwest of McLaughlin, 4.51 2 miles north-northwest of Mobridge, 3.85 at the Mobridge Municipal Airport, and 3.84 in Timber Lake. In response to this heavy rain, Oak Creek near Wakpala reached minor flood stage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Oak Creek near Wakpala, at the WKPS2 gauge, rose into minor flood stage of 10.0 feet at 3:00 pm MDT on June 25th, crested at 11.8 feet at 7:45 pm MDT on the 25th, and then fell below flood stage at 7:15 am MDT on the 26th. Agricultural land was impacted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure deepened as it moved from eastern Colorado on April 4 to central Minnesota on April 5. Slightly warmer and drier air aloft east of the upper-level low track kept any more persistent measurable precipitation mainly from the James River valley westward, but also limited the type to mainly light freezing rain and freezing drizzle except for more elevated areas west of the James River.  Several locations had ice accumulation from one-tenth to one-quarter inch, with a narrow zone from 2 to 5 inches of snow from Chamberlain to Wessington.  Northerly winds gusted to 30 to 45 mph as precipitation was coming to an end, but the overall lack of snowfall kept visibility from being significantly reduced.

While most remained sub-severe, wind gusts of 58 to 63 mph were recorded in Spink County prior to the weakening of this activity during the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The South Dakota Department of Transportation weather station near Mellette recorded a wind gust of 63 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed along a stationary front during peak heating during the afternoon across portions of the Prairie Coteau and James River Valley. A brief landspout tornado was reported in the Clark area between roughly 2:30 pm and 2:45 pm CDT.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A landspout tornado was observed north of the Clark County airport to the northeast of Clark.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak low pressure system served as the focal point for small, scattered and slow-moving thunderstorms across far northeastern South Dakota during the afternoon of June 5th. A brief landspout tornado was observed north of Summit and south of Sisseton around 2:45 pm. No damage was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The report of a brief landspout tornado came with a photograph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated and small slow-moving showers developed across northeastern South Dakota along an elongated area of weak low pressure during peak afternoon heating amidst upper 80s and low 90s temperatures. A brief landspout tornado was observed 9 miles north of Rauville around 4:00 pm. No damage was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A photo of the landspout was submitted via social media.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of a deep snowpack, a liquid equivalent exceeding 4 inches, and frozen soils due to persistently below-normal spring temperatures set the stage for flooding along the Big Sioux. However, a significant rise in temperatures, well above normal, triggered rapid snowmelt. The resulting runoff swiftly made its way into the Big Sioux River and its associated tributaries.||The Big Sioux river headwaters near Florence set a record flood stage. Points downstream along the Big Sioux River crested at moderate to major flood stage. Many local and county roads and bridges were impacted by the high water, along with widespread inundation of agricultural lands. ||A Presidential Disaster Declaration was approved for Brown, Clark, Codington, Day, Faulk, Grant, Hand, Marshall, Potter and Roberts Counties, as well as for the Lake Traverse Indian Reservation, where an estimated $2,305,362 in total qualifying costs were incurred during spring flooding. ||Apart from the mainstem river, there was heightened concern regarding Lake Kampeska. The Watertown Water Filtration plant and numerous local residences faced threats from both elevated lake water levels and the potential for ice breakup and shoves. In response, residents initiated sandbagging operations to safeguard their properties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Big Sioux river near Castlewood crested at 11.65 feet, well into moderate flood stage. This overtopped a road in addition to flooding agricultural lands.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Flooding on the James River which started in mid-April continued through the entirety of June, due to the combination of April snowmelt and rounds of heavy showers and thunderstorms during the middle to end of the month. Agricultural land and some county roadways continued to be significantly impacted.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The James River near Columbia, CMBS2, started the month in moderate flood stage at 17.31 feet, then steadily dropped through the month. It reached minor flood stage of <16 feet from June 20th through early on June 24th before rising back into moderate flood stage thereafter due to heavy rains, and remained in moderate flood stage through the rest of the month. Significant amounts of agricultural lands remained flooded through the month.

Agricultural land and some county roadways continued to be significantly impacted.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The James River near Stratford, SFDS2, started the month in moderate flood stage at 17.32 feet, then steadily dropped through the month. It reached minor flood stage of <17 feet on June 11th, and remained in there through the rest of the month. Significant amounts of agricultural lands remained flooded through the month, and county roads were flooded.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The James River near Ashton, ATNS2, started the month in moderate flood stage at 15.42 feet, then steadily dropped through the month. It reached minor flood stage of <14 feet on June 12th, and then remained there through the rest of the month. Significant amounts of farm land were flooded, including crop and pasture land.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Just 4 days after a major winter storm at the end of March, a strong low pressure system developed across the western Great Plains and strengthened as it tracked northeast into Iowa on the afternoon of April 4th and into northeastern Minnesota by noon on April 5th. This system produced heavy snow from late in the evening on April 3rd through early in the morning on April 5th across central and northeastern South Dakota, along with northeast winds of 15-30mph. The initial snow over east central South Dakota transitioned to a freezing rain or wintry mix for a period of time during the late afternoon and evening hours before switching back to snow. Visibilities were frequently reduced to between a mile and a quarter mile in heavy snow, with localized blizzard conditions at times. Winds shifted to the west-northwest on April 5th and gusted to between 30 and 45 mph, which led to continued significantly reduced visibilities at times through the day despite the lack of new falling snow. Ditches were full of snow from this and previous snowfall events, which allowed for significant drifts to develop on roadways. Some of the highest snowfall totals included 18 inches 7 miles east of Hayes, 16 inches 8 miles south-southwest of Seneca, 15.6 inches a mile west of Roscoe, 15 inches 9 miles east of Grey Goose, 4 miles northwest of Onida and 11 miles northeast of Pierre, and 14 inches in Bowdle, 5 miles northwest of Mission Ridge and 3 miles northwest of Westport.||Many US and state highways were either impassable, closed or under no travel advised statements from the South Dakota Department of Transportation. Interstate 29 closed from Watertown to the North Dakota boarder from 2pm April 4th through 7am April 6th. Business and school closures were widespread as a result. Governor Kristi Noem ordered the closure of state government executive branch offices from April 4th through 5th for numerous counties. Additionally, there were several reports of power outages, and ranchers were severely impacted due to the storm's overlap with the calving season. Finally, the roof of a downtown Aberdeen building, Dacotah Bank, began to collapse on the evening of April 6th due to the weight of the heavy snow. Following this storm, many locations had observed a top-10 winter season for total snowfall. April snow depth records were shattered at Aberdeen and Sisseton as well. All the snow and much below average temperatures to start April set the stage for significant spring flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm total 18 inches of snow was observed 7 miles east of Hayes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The snowfall during the season in northeast and central South Dakota exceeded normal levels, with many locations ranking among the top 10 for the snowiest seasons. Additionally, the late spring conditions were unusually cold, resulting in a persistent and exceptionally deep snowpack until early April. The depth of the snowpack ranged from 15 to 30 plus inches, containing approximately 4 to 8 inches of snow water equivalent. However, the weather pattern shifted to abnormally warm conditions in the middle of April. Starting from April 9th, temperatures rapidly rose into the 40s and 50s in areas with deep snowpack, and occasionally reached the 60s and 70s thereafter. The rapid snowmelt that followed resulted in extensive overland flooding in the region between April 10th and 18th.||Flooding resulted in severe damage to public infrastructure. Several reports surfaced of county and township roads being washed out or submerged in water across central and northeastern South Dakota. The communities of Wilmot, SD, and Corona, SD, experienced significant and widespread flooding. In Grant County, SD, a water rescue was carried out after a motorist drove into a flooded roadway. Furthermore, flooding impacted infrastructure along Big Stone Lake in Grant County, Lake Kampeska in Codington County, and the Lake Traverse Reservation. A Presidential Disaster Declaration was approved for Brown, Clark, Codington, Day, Faulk, Grant, Hand, Marshall, Potter and Roberts Counties, as well as for the Lake Traverse Indian Reservation, where an estimated $2,305,362 in total qualifying costs were incurred.
A strong wind gradient around a large upper level circulation resulted in widespread winds of 30 to 40 mph and gusts from 45 to 65 mph east of the James River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Northwest winds gusted as high as 53 mph at the South Dakota Road Weather Information System site SD519 10 miles west of Davis, with sustained winds 30 to 35 mph at Automated Weather Observing System site K7G9 in Canton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of a deep snowpack, a liquid equivalent exceeding 4 inches, and frozen soils due to persistently below-normal spring temperatures set the stage for flooding along the Big Sioux. However, a significant rise in temperatures, well above normal, triggered rapid snowmelt. The resulting runoff swiftly made its way into the Big Sioux River and its associated tributaries.||The Big Sioux river headwaters near Florence set a record flood stage. Points downstream along the Big Sioux River crested at moderate to major flood stage. Many local and county roads and bridges were impacted by the high water, along with widespread inundation of agricultural lands. ||A Presidential Disaster Declaration was approved for Brown, Clark, Codington, Day, Faulk, Grant, Hand, Marshall, Potter and Roberts Counties, as well as for the Lake Traverse Indian Reservation, where an estimated $2,305,362 in total qualifying costs were incurred during spring flooding. ||Apart from the mainstem river, there was heightened concern regarding Lake Kampeska. The Watertown Water Filtration plant and numerous local residences faced threats from both elevated lake water levels and the potential for ice breakup and shoves. In response, residents initiated sandbagging operations to safeguard their properties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Willow Creek near Watertown hit flood stage in the early afternoon of April 11th, increasing to 15.90 feet later during the overnight hours. This is 5.9 feet above flood stage and mainly affected agricultural lands.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rapid snowmelt from a few day period of well above normal temperatures focused on the 11-13th of April resulted in minor to locally moderate flooding through much of the Big Sioux River Basin. Some areas north of I-90 lost between 2 to 5 inches of snow water equivalent (SWE) from the first to second week of April, but antecedent drought conditions resulted in only a percentage of this water actually making it into the river system.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Big Sioux River near Richland, SD rose above flood stage on the morning of April 12th before cresting around 1.36 feet above flood stage (1118.36 feet) on April 15th. The river then fell below flood stage on the evening of April 16th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure deepened as it moved from eastern Colorado on April 4 to central Minnesota on April 5. Slightly warmer and drier air aloft east of the upper-level low track kept any more persistent measurable precipitation mainly from the James River valley westward, but also limited the type to mainly light freezing rain and freezing drizzle except for more elevated areas west of the James River.  Several locations had ice accumulation from one-tenth to one-quarter inch, with a narrow zone from 2 to 5 inches of snow from Chamberlain to Wessington.  Northerly winds gusted to 30 to 45 mph as precipitation was coming to an end, but the overall lack of snowfall kept visibility from being significantly reduced.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A persistent period of light freezing rain and freezing drizzle resulted in ice accumulation to one-quarter inch on elevated surfaces. Several power outages occurred in areas near Fedora and Carthage between 2000LST April 4 and 0030LST April 5 as winds increased to a gusty 30 to 40 mph by evening. Cold temperatures the following day kept ice present on surfaces well into April 5.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper-level wave moving through southwest flow aloft, acting on an environment with 1000 to 1500 J/kg of MUCAPE in the vicinity of a surface trough, resulted in scattered thunderstorms across far southeast South Dakota, far northeast Nebraska, northwest Iowa, and southwest Minnesota during the evening of April 19th. The strongest storms produced severe hail ranging from 1.0 to 1.5 inches in diameter.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail in Elk Point ranged from 1.0 to 1.5 inches in diameter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching upper level jet within the presence of a sharp lower- to mid-level boundary focused widespread convective development during the overnight hours.  Elevated instability of 1000-1500 J/kg and deep layer shear in excess of 40 kts produced a very organized bowing system, which resulted in widespread 60 to 80 mph wind reports in southeast South Dakota, northeast Nebraska, and northwest Iowa.  At the peak, several thousand people were without power.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts as high as 59 mph were measured by South Dakota Road Weather Information System site SD605 3 miles east-southeast of Parker.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Conditions averaged warm (3 to 5 degrees above normal) and dry (50-75 percent of normal) across southeast South Dakota during June and resulted in up to a slight worsening of drought conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate (D1) drought conditions worsened to severe (D2) during mid-June, and was briefly extreme (D3) late month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Linear storms developed near a frontal boundary, moving across southeast South Dakota and into northwest Iowa and southwest Minnesota early on June 24. Within an environment of shear, a strong low-level jet, and strong DCAPE, some storms produced severe-level winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts up to 58 mph were measured by the Automated Surface Observing System site KFSD 3 miles north of Sioux Falls.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts removed a roof from a building on the north side of Yankton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated storms formed within the instability axis ahead of a frontal zone in central South Dakota. With up to 2000 J/kg MLCAPE and 30-35 knots of deep-layer shear, a few storms took on brief supercell structures and produced large hail.

Isolated thunderstorms developed along a weak cold front near the James River Valley.  An environment of MLCAPE up to 1500 J/kg and DCAPE of around 1000 J/kg allowed stronger storms to produce gusty winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds uprooted a tree.  Hail up to dime size combined with the wind to result in damage to crops.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abundant moisture, relatively weak air flow and a weak disturbance moving through the region produced scattered thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening of June 10.  Water loaded storms produced locally heavy rainfall and some flooding. In addition, the developing phase of storms across southwest Minnesota resulted in very brief landspouts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds snapped several 4 inch tree branches.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts up to 61 mph were measured at a personal weather station in Tabor.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon and early evening thunderstorms focused along a weak surface boundary from southwest Minnesota into areas south of Interstate 90 in southeast South Dakota.  Even with very weak deep-layer shear, modest surface-based instability of 1500-2000 J/kg led to the rapid development of storms, which peaked in mainly hail producing severity early in the life cycle, as well as an erratic tornado in eastern Bon Homme County.

A strong upper-level wave across northeastern South Dakota ignited isolated afternoon thunderstorms near a cold front across south central South Dakota. Storms thrived and became briefly supercellular in a highly sheared and unstable environment while tracking eastward south of South Dakota Highway 46 into the early evening.  After a brief decrease in intensity entering Iowa, storms strengthened once again mid to late evening moving east of Cherokee generally along and south of Iowa Highway 3 with a variety of severe weather types.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds heavily damaged a barn and split several trees on an acreage. Property damage costs are a rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong high pressure and elevated near surface moisture resulted in a multi-day heat wave across southeast South Dakota, southwest Minnesota, northwest Iowa, and northeast Nebraska from July 25-28. With air temperatures rising into the 90s and low 100s and dewpoint temperatures in the 70s and low 80s, afternoon and evening heat indices generally peaked from 100 to 120 degrees with little overnight recovery. The most widespread opressive conditions occurred during the afternoons and evenings of July 26 and July 27.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Daily afternoon and evening heat indices ranged from 100 to 110 degrees with a maximum heat index of 110 degrees at the Automated Weather Observation System site at the Madison Municipal Airport on July 27.

An isolated storm developed in the axis of greatest instability consisting of 1500+ J/kg MLCAPE and 1000 J/kg DCAPE.  For a couple hours, a balance of shear and instability supported a long track of damaging winds and spotty large hail from near Miller southeast to near Beresford. Measured winds from 60 to 80 mph were common.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts as high as 63 mph were measured by a personal weather station 5 miles north-northeast of Stickney.

An approaching upper level jet within the presence of a sharp lower- to mid-level boundary focused widespread convective development during the overnight hours.  Elevated instability of 1000-1500 J/kg and deep layer shear in excess of 40 kts produced a very organized bowing system, which resulted in widespread 60 to 80 mph wind reports in southeast South Dakota, northeast Nebraska, and northwest Iowa.  At the peak, several thousand people were without power.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple personal weather system sites around Wagner recorded peak wind gusts between 59 and 62 mph.

Afternoon and early evening thunderstorms focused along a weak surface boundary from southwest Minnesota into areas south of Interstate 90 in southeast South Dakota.  Even with very weak deep-layer shear, modest surface-based instability of 1500-2000 J/kg led to the rapid development of storms, which peaked in mainly hail producing severity early in the life cycle, as well as an erratic tornado in eastern Bon Homme County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado spun up quickly at 1504 CST beneath a rapidly intensifying storm just north of 302nd Street in an open field between 425th and 426th Avenue. The tornado crawled through open country toward the north-northwest for several minutes and crossed Beaver Creek before damaging several trees on a farmstead along 425th Avenue. The tornado also ripped tin off an abandoned outbuilding. At this point, the tornado track veered to the north as it moved through soybean fields and across 301st Street before it lifted around a tenth of a mile north of 301st Street. The average path width for the tornado was 30 yards. The tornado was rated an EF-0 with a maximum estimated wind gust of 74 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Limited instability and shear led to rapidly developing and dissipating storms. The strongest storms briefly produced large hail, with some very large observed in southeast South Dakota near Salem.

An approaching upper level jet within the presence of a sharp lower- to mid-level boundary focused widespread convective development during the overnight hours.  Elevated instability of 1000-1500 J/kg and deep layer shear in excess of 40 kts produced a very organized bowing system, which resulted in widespread 60 to 80 mph wind reports in southeast South Dakota, northeast Nebraska, and northwest Iowa.  At the peak, several thousand people were without power.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts as high as 60 mph were measured by South Dakota Road Weather Information System site SD533 4 miles west of Plankinton.

Thunderstorms developed along a dryline and brought severe weather across central to northeastern South Dakota during the mid-afternoon hours. Large hail, between quarter and golf ball size, was the main threat, along with isolated severe wind gusts. Hail nearly covered the ground near Fort Sisseton. Tree branches, 4 inches in diameter, were downed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail, some larger than a quarter size, nearly covered the ground.

An isolated storm developed in the axis of greatest instability consisting of 1500+ J/kg MLCAPE and 1000 J/kg DCAPE.  For a couple hours, a balance of shear and instability supported a long track of damaging winds and spotty large hail from near Miller southeast to near Beresford. Measured winds from 60 to 80 mph were common.

Afternoon and early evening thunderstorms focused along a weak surface boundary from southwest Minnesota into areas south of Interstate 90 in southeast South Dakota.  Even with very weak deep-layer shear, modest surface-based instability of 1500-2000 J/kg led to the rapid development of storms, which peaked in mainly hail producing severity early in the life cycle, as well as an erratic tornado in eastern Bon Homme County.

Late afternoon thunderstorms developed in the exit region of a strong jet streak in northwest flow aloft, focused along a prefrontal trough across southeast South Dakota. Storms quickly became severe, with MLCAPE of up to 1500 J/kg and deep layer shear around 40 knots.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Most hail was from dime to nickel size, but the largest stones were the size of quarters.

Thunderstorms developed along a dryline and brought severe weather across central to northeastern South Dakota during the mid-afternoon hours. Large hail, between quarter and golf ball size, was the main threat, along with isolated severe wind gusts. Hail nearly covered the ground near Fort Sisseton. Hail also covered the ground, up to three inches deep in some spots, six miles east of Carpenter. Penny to quarter-size hail and 55 mph wind gusts were reported eight miles east-southeast of Rosseau. Tree branches, 4 inches in diameter, were downed.

With air temperatures rising into the 90s and low 100s and dewpoint temperatures in the 70s and low 80s, afternoon and evening heat indices generally peaked from 100 to 120 degrees with little overnight recovery. The James River from the North Dakota and South Dakota border to near Ashton remained above flood stage through July. The river slowly decreased through the month due to below-average rainfall. Agricultural land and some county roadways continued to be impacted.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The James River near Columbia, CMBS2, started the month in moderate flood stage at 16.3 feet, then saw a slight increase to 16.5 feet due to heavy rainfall in early July. The river dropped to minor flood stage of <16 feet early on July 15th, with a steady decline for the rest of the month. Significant amounts of agricultural land remained flooded throughout the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching upper level jet within the presence of a sharp lower- to mid-level boundary focused widespread convective development during the overnight hours.  Elevated instability of 1000-1500 J/kg and deep layer shear in excess of 40 kts produced a very organized bowing system, which resulted in widespread 60 to 80 mph wind reports in southeast South Dakota, northeast Nebraska, and northwest Iowa.  At the peak, several thousand people were without power.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts as high as 80 mph were measured by South Dakota Road Weather Information System site SD508 3 miles south-southwest of Beresford.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snowmelt flooding from April continued into May along the very slowly draining James River in South Dakota. While flood levels decreased from their crest in April, minor to moderate flooding persisted, only slowly falling through May. This flooding mainly impacted agricultural land.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding continued from April along the James River near Mitchell, SD with very slowly falling levels through the month. Flood levels were in the Moderate category for 4 days and Minor stage for 31 days. Flooding continued into June.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snowmelt flooding from April continued into May along the very slowly draining James River in South Dakota. While flood levels decreased from their crest in April, minor to moderate flooding persisted, only slowly falling through May. This flooding mainly impacted agricultural land.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding continued from April along the James River near Scotland, SD with very slowly falling levels through the month. Flood levels were in the Moderate category for 9 days and Minor stage for 12 days. The river fell below flood stage on the morning of May 12th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Despite temperatures which averaged 4 to 6 degrees warmer than normal and precipitation which was below normal on average, locally heavier precipitation amounts allowed for a small improvement to the worst drought conditions across southeast South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme (D3) drought conditions improved to severe (D2) drought conditions by mid-May.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak boundary slid southeast into the region during the midday and afternoon hours. Isolated storms formed along the convergence zone with marginal deep-layer shear up to 30 knots and MLCAPE of 500-1000 J/kg, which supported brief multicell development.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Occasional pea to dime size hail covered the ground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front brought the threat of severe thunderstorms to northeast South Dakota on the afternoon and evening of August 10th. Initially, individual storms presented both a hail and wind threat in the James Valley, with reports of golfball sized hail in Brown and Hand counties. As the storms moved east into the Prairie Coteau region, they organized into a broken line and presented more of a wind threat. The Department of Transportation mesonet station 6 miles west of Henry recorded a 74 mph wind gust, and the nearby town of Henry also experienced damage to trees. There was also a pair of brief tornado touchdowns farther to the south associated with one of the stronger cells embedded within this line, though no damage was ever noted.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement and a storm chaser reported the brief touchdown of a tornado in an open field. The tornado path was later visible on hi-resolution satellite imagery.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snowmelt flooding from April continued into May along the very slowly draining James River in South Dakota. While flood levels decreased from their crest in April, minor to moderate flooding persisted, only slowly falling through May. This flooding mainly impacted agricultural land.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding continued from April along the James River near Forestburg, SD with very slowly falling levels through the month. Flood levels were in the Moderate category for 5 days and Minor stage for 31 days. Flooding continued into June.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snowmelt resultant flooding from April continued through May and into June along the very slowly draining James River in South Dakota. While flood levels continued to decrease from their crest in April, minor flooding persisted into June in some locations, slowly falling through the month. This flooding mainly impacted agricultural land.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding continued from May along the James River near Mitchell, SD with very slowly falling levels through the month. The peak river level was 1.15 feet above flood stage (18.15 ft) occurring early in the day on the 1st, before falling below flood stage early on the 14th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper-level wave across northeastern South Dakota ignited isolated afternoon thunderstorms near a cold front across south central South Dakota. Storms thrived and became briefly supercellular in a highly sheared and unstable environment while tracking eastward south of South Dakota Highway 46 into the early evening.  After a brief decrease in intensity entering Iowa, storms strengthened once again mid to late evening moving east of Cherokee generally along and south of Iowa Highway 3 with a variety of severe weather types.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to walnut size was accompanied by heavy rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Conditions averaged warm (3 to 5 degrees above normal) and dry (50-75 percent of normal) across southeast South Dakota during June and resulted in up to a slight worsening of drought conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate (D1) drought conditions worsened to severe (D2) conditions during early June, and then to extreme (D3) by the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Conditions averaged warm (3 to 5 degrees above normal) and dry (50-75 percent of normal) across southeast South Dakota during June and resulted in up to a slight worsening of drought conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate (D1) drought conditions worsened to severe (D2) conditions during early June, and then to extreme (D3) by the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm developed over eastern portions of Oglala Lakota County, producing large hail near Sharps Corner.

A weak boundary slid southeast into the region during the midday and afternoon hours. Isolated storms formed along the convergence zone with marginal deep-layer shear up to 30 knots and MLCAPE of 500-1000 J/kg, which supported brief multicell development.

A strong upper-level wave across northeastern South Dakota ignited isolated afternoon thunderstorms near a cold front across south central South Dakota. Storms thrived and became briefly supercellular in a highly sheared and unstable environment while tracking eastward south of South Dakota Highway 46 into the early evening.  After a brief decrease in intensity entering Iowa, storms strengthened once again mid to late evening moving east of Cherokee generally along and south of Iowa Highway 3 with a variety of severe weather types.

A heat dome to the south resulted in temperatures topping out in the 90s to just over 100 degrees, with dewpoints in the 70s to low 80s, generating heat index values in excess of 100 degrees for a three day period. During this timeframe, most weather stations reported heat index values falling below 75 degrees for at least a few hours in the morning, however.|During the afternoon on Monday, moisture pooling along a weak warm front resulted in the highest heat index values across central South Dakota. The Remote Automatic Weather Station (RAWS) in southeast Hand County reported a peak heat index of 118 degrees, while Pierre Regional Airport registered a peak heat index of 116 in the afternoon.|Heat index values again topped above 100 degrees for much of central and northeast South Dakota on Tuesday as that weak warm front shifted northeast slightly. The highest heat index values were focused across Grant and southern Roberts counties, with a total of 20 different weather stations recording a heat index between 111 and 136 degrees in Grant County alone. The Automated Weather Observing System (AWOS) 3 miles northeast of Milbank recorded a temperature of 93 and a dewpoint of 86, resulting in a heat index of 125, and while nearby stations were higher, we do not know the citing details of these individual stations.|The heat and humidity eased slightly for Wednesday with a broader coverage of 100 to 112 degree heat index values. Even north central South Dakota, which had seen cloud cover during the two days prior, witnessed heat index values above 105 degrees. This period of excessive heat wasn't alleviated until a cold front moved through Wednesday night.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The tail end of large scale forcing associated with an upper-level low moving across North Dakota brushed through far eastern South Dakota, southwest Minnesota, and northwest Iowa just after diurnal heating maximum. With a weak moisture convergence axis and 500-1000 J/kg MLCAPE in the presence of 40 knots of deep-layer shear, isolated multicell storms developed which produced marginally large hail during the very brief life cycle.

A weak boundary slid southeast into the region during the midday and afternoon hours. Isolated storms formed along the convergence zone with marginal deep-layer shear up to 30 knots and MLCAPE of 500-1000 J/kg, which supported brief multicell development.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A significant accumulation of pea to dime size hail occurred.

A cold front brought the threat of severe thunderstorms to northeast South Dakota on the afternoon and evening of August 10th. Initially, individual storms presented both a hail and wind threat in the James Valley, with reports of golfball sized hail in Brown and Hand counties. As the storms moved east into the Prairie Coteau region, they organized into a broken line and presented more of a wind threat. The Department of Transportation mesonet station 6 miles west of Henry recorded a 74 mph wind gust, and the nearby town of Henry also experienced damage to trees. There was also a pair of brief tornado touchdowns farther to the south associated with one of the stronger cells embedded within this line, though no damage was ever noted.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A member of the public was contacted and they estimated the wind speed at 65 mph.

After heavy rainfall during the first half of August, the James river gauge at Ashton rose above flood stage on the morning of August 11th and remained in flood through August 21st. The river crested at 13.25 on August 18th, 0.25 feet above flood stage. This only impacted already previously flooded crop and pastures.
Portions of the James river, originally in flood since mid April, continued to slowly drop during the month of August.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The James river gauge at Columbia observed a level of 16.0 feet on August 1st. Over the course of the month the river level dropped slowly, finally falling below the flood stage of 13.0 feet on the morning of August 23rd, 130 days after the initial start of flooding. Flooding continued to mostly impact cropland and pastures, along with a few county roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Portions of the James river, originally in flood since mid April, continued to slowly drop during the month of August.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The James river gauge at Stratford measured 16.0 feet, 2.0 feet above flood stage at the start of the month. By the end of the August the gauge was still in flood at 14.7 feet. Flooding continued to mostly impact cropland and pastures, along with a few county roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak front moving across North Dakota helped initiate a few storms in north central South Dakota during the overnight hours. One of these storms was strong enough to generate hail and gusty winds that resulted in locally significant crop damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail to the size of quarters combined with wind gusts of 50 to 60 mph resulted in significant crop damage 2 mines north northeast of Hillsview.

Sisseton, for example, recorded 6.45 inches of moisture for the month, which is 3.84 inches above normal.
