EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A heat dome to the south resulted in temperatures topping out in the 90s to just over 100 degrees, with dewpoints in the 70s to low 80s, generating heat index values in excess of 100 degrees for a three day period. During this timeframe, most weather stations reported heat index values falling below 75 degrees for at least a few hours in the morning, however.|During the afternoon on Monday, moisture pooling along a weak warm front resulted in the highest heat index values across central South Dakota. The Remote Automatic Weather Station (RAWS) in southeast Hand County reported a peak heat index of 118 degrees, while Pierre Regional Airport registered a peak heat index of 116 in the afternoon.|Heat index values again topped above 100 degrees for much of central and northeast South Dakota on Tuesday as that weak warm front shifted northeast slightly. The highest heat index values were focused across Grant and southern Roberts counties, with a total of 20 different weather stations recording a heat index between 111 and 136 degrees in Grant County alone. The Automated Weather Observing System (AWOS) 3 miles northeast of Milbank recorded a temperature of 93 and a dewpoint of 86, resulting in a heat index of 125, and while nearby stations were higher, we do not know the citing details of these individual stations.|The heat and humidity eased slightly for Wednesday with a broader coverage of 100 to 112 degree heat index values. Even north central South Dakota, which had seen cloud cover during the two days prior, witnessed heat index values above 105 degrees. This period of excessive heat wasn't alleviated until a cold front moved through Wednesday night.

A cold front brought the threat of severe thunderstorms to northeast South Dakota on the afternoon and evening of August 10th. Initially, individual storms presented both a hail and wind threat in the James Valley, with reports of golfball sized hail in Brown and Hand counties. As the storms moved east into the Prairie Coteau region, they organized into a broken line and presented more of a wind threat. The Department of Transportation mesonet station 6 miles west of Henry recorded a 74 mph wind gust, and the nearby town of Henry also experienced damage to trees. There was also a pair of brief tornado touchdowns farther to the south associated with one of the stronger cells embedded within this line, though no damage was ever noted.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported wind damage to his corn field.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Wildfire smoke from northwest Canada followed the airflow around high pressure over the Rockies. Low level smoke moved into South Dakota initially on August 28th, however lowest visibilities and highest Air Quality Index (AQI) values were observed as a backdoor cold concentrated the smoke along its axis on August 29th. That front pushed back across the area as a warm front late on the 30th of August into the morning of August 31st, after which a slow gradual improvement was noted. AQI in Aberdeen topped out at 107, Watertown at 95, and Pierre at 92. Visibility was impacted for over 56 total hours in both Aberdeen and Britton, 50 hours in Redfield, 39 hours in Gettysburg, 33 hours in Mobridge, and 22 hours in Pierre.

A strong upper-level wave across northeastern South Dakota ignited isolated afternoon thunderstorms near a cold front across south central South Dakota. Storms thrived and became briefly supercellular in a highly sheared and unstable environment while tracking eastward south of South Dakota Highway 46 into the early evening.  After a brief decrease in intensity entering Iowa, storms strengthened once again mid to late evening moving east of Cherokee generally along and south of Iowa Highway 3 with a variety of severe weather types.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snowmelt resultant flooding from April continued through May and into June along the very slowly draining James River in South Dakota. While flood levels continued to decrease from their crest in April, minor flooding persisted into June in some locations, slowly falling through the month. This flooding mainly impacted agricultural land.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding continued from May along the James River near Mitchell, SD with very slowly falling levels through the month. The peak river level was 1.15 feet above flood stage (18.15 ft) occurring early in the day on the 1st, before falling below flood stage early on the 14th.

The storm produced hail larger than golf ball size and eventually strong wind gusts on the plains.

Snowmelt resultant flooding from April continued through May and into June along the very slowly draining James River in South Dakota. While flood levels continued to decrease from their crest in April, minor flooding persisted into June in some locations, slowly falling through the month. This flooding mainly impacted agricultural land.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding continued from May along the James River near Huron, SD with very slowly falling levels through the month. The peak river level was 0.83 feet above flood stage (11.83 ft) occurring early in the day on the 1st, slowly falling for the remainder of the month. Flooding continued into June.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding continued from May along the James River near Mitchell, SD with very slowly falling levels through the month. The peak river level was 1.15 feet above flood stage (18.15 ft) occurring early in the day on the 1st, before falling below flood stage early on the 14th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Instability which reached around 1000 J/kg of MLCAPE in an environment of weak deep-layer flow allowed thunderstorm cores to collapse and produce mainly damaging wind.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds tipped over a semi-truck near mile marker 36 on Interstate 29.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arc of convection developed ahead of a slow-moving upper-level low across central Nebraska. With around 2000 J/kg MUCAPE in a narrow zone and around 40 knots of deep layer shear, a few rotating severe storms developed with enhanced wind.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gust was measured at the station in North Sioux City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Conditions averaged warm (3 to 5 degrees above normal) and dry (50-75 percent of normal) across southeast South Dakota during June and resulted in up to a slight worsening of drought conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate (D1) drought conditions worsened to severe (D2) conditions during early June, and then to extreme (D3) by the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper-level wave across northeastern South Dakota ignited isolated afternoon thunderstorms near a cold front across south central South Dakota. Storms thrived and became briefly supercellular in a highly sheared and unstable environment while tracking eastward south of South Dakota Highway 46 into the early evening.  After a brief decrease in intensity entering Iowa, storms strengthened once again mid to late evening moving east of Cherokee generally along and south of Iowa Highway 3 with a variety of severe weather types.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large hail up to half dollar size was shown in social media picture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Despite temperatures which averaged 4 to 6 degrees warmer than normal and precipitation which was below normal on average, locally heavier precipitation amounts allowed for a small improvement to the worst drought conditions across southeast South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) drought conditions improved to moderate (D1) conditions by mid-May.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two areas of thunderstorms brought severe weather to central and northeast South Dakota on the evening of July 25th. A warm front across northeastern South Dakota provided the focus for storms that generated at least one observed wind gust of 63 mph. A secondary area of thunderstorms developed over central South Dakota near a surface low-pressure system. The thunderstorms that developed over Hand County resulted in quarter-size hail, winds in excess of 60 mph, and a brief tornado that produced EF0 damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief tornado resulted in the collapse of the walls of a 25X50 shed. The debris from the shed was spread roughly 75 yards to the southeast. There was no other visible damage to the property or shelterbelt. A neighbor to the southeast witnessed clear rotation extending from the cloud base in the direction of where the damaged occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A late day frontal zone moved into an unstable airmass with up to 2000 J/kg MUCAPE and sparked several severe thunderstorms, which produced large hail up to golf ball size. In addition, a couple of brief tornadic spin ups occurred across Kingsbury County, but resulted in no damage.

An expanding upper-level ridge across the southern and central U.S. pushed heat into the region, as moisture pooled just ahead of an advancing frontal zone.  The combination of heat and higher dewpoints resulted in afternoon and early evening heat indices from 100 to 115 degrees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A maximum heat index of 110 degrees was reached at South Dakota Road Weather Information System site SD502 near Alexandria.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Temperatures averaged 3 to 5 degrees above normal during September, with precipitation varying between 25 to 75 percent of normal east of the James River to around normal in south central South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) drought conditions worsened to extreme (D3) drought in mid September.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a strong mid-level jet streak pushed northeast ahead of an upper-level low pressure area in southwest South Dakota, numerous thunderstorms developed along a weak surface boundary during the mid to late afternoon hours in far eastern South Dakota.  While instability was somewhat limited at a maximum of around 1000 J/kg, a very low LCL and concentration of 0-1 km bulk shear supported the development of four brief tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm chaser witnessed a very brief tornado along 479th Avenue just south of the intersection with 197th Street, around 6 miles north of White. The touchdown was evident by swirling field debris, but no damage indicator was present to allow for a rating of the tornado.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong ridge of high pressure persisted through the Central and Southern Plains at the start of September and brought hot temperatures to the region with highs in the 90s to around 100 degrees. Dewpoint readings were somewhat lower than late August, mainly in the 60s, which kept the more extreme heat at bay.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Over a two-day period from the afternoon of September 2 through early evening on September 3, a maximum heat index of 102 degrees was reached at South Dakota Road Weather Information System site SD597 near Howard.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tremendous amount of nickel size hail fell which shredded soybean and corn leaves.  A few hailstones that fell were as large as a quarter. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An immense ridge of high pressure over the Central Plains and Mississippi Valley entrenched heat and high dewpoint air across the region, resulting in daily heat indices from 100 to around 120, with very little recovery during the overnight hours. One direct fatality occurred when an infant was left in a vehicle for an extended period of time during the day in Yankton.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Daily afternoon and evening heat indices ranged from 100 to 118 degrees with a maximum heat index of 118 degrees at a South Dakota State University Mesonet site in Parkston on August 21, 114 degrees at a South Dakota Road Weather Information System site near Freeman on August 23, and 111 degrees near Tripp on August 21.

Afternoon and early evening thunderstorms focused along a weak surface boundary from southwest Minnesota into areas south of Interstate 90 in southeast South Dakota.  Even with very weak deep-layer shear, modest surface-based instability of 1500-2000 J/kg led to the rapid development of storms, which peaked in mainly hail producing severity early in the life cycle, as well as an erratic tornado in eastern Bon Homme County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from very heavy rainfall was flowing over 213th Street between 404th and 405th Avenue.  Rainfall amounts in the immediate area ranged from 5 to 7 inches in less than two hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed along a frontal boundary during the mid to late afternoon as convergence moved from far eastern South Dakota into southwest Minnesota. A decent amount of mid-level instability resulted in MLCAPE up to 1500 J/kg while deep-layer shear remained largely less than 30 kts. This environment favored storms that produced large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail was mostly nickel to quarter size in Elkton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a strong mid-level jet streak pushed northeast ahead of an upper-level low pressure area in southwest South Dakota, numerous thunderstorms developed along a weak surface boundary during the mid to late afternoon hours in far eastern South Dakota. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple storm chasers reported development of swirling debris at the surface below a funnel cloud north of 205th Street and east of 468th Avenue around 3 miles east-southeast of Bruce.  Debris was witnessed near the surface as the slow-moving tornado tracked for a few minutes northeast through an open field. The tornado lifted just south of 204th Street.  The average path width was estimated at 15 yards with a maximum width of 20 yards. However, the lack of damage indicators did not allow for a rating of this tornado.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Late afternoon thunderstorms developed in the exit region of a strong jet streak in northwest flow aloft, focused along a prefrontal trough across southeast South Dakota. Storms quickly became severe, with MLCAPE of up to 1500 J/kg and deep layer shear around 40 knots.

Another in a series of jet streaks in northwest flow aloft initiated afternoon and early evening convection across eastern South Dakota, southwest Minnesota, and northwest Iowa.  Several storms in an environment of 1500-2000 MLCAPE and around 40 knots of distributed deep-layer shear took on supercellular characteristics and produced hail up to baseball size in southwest Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper-level wave across northeastern South Dakota ignited isolated afternoon thunderstorms near a cold front across south central South Dakota. Storms thrived and became briefly supercellular in a highly sheared and unstable environment while tracking eastward south of South Dakota Highway 46 into the early evening.  After a brief decrease in intensity entering Iowa, storms strengthened once again mid to late evening moving east of Cherokee generally along and south of Iowa Highway 3 with a variety of severe weather types.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An expanding upper-level ridge across the southern and central U.S. pushed heat into the region, as moisture pooled just ahead of an advancing frontal zone.  The combination of heat and higher dewpoints resulted in afternoon and early evening heat indices from 100 to 115 degrees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A maximum heat index of 111 degrees was reached at Automated Weather Observing System site KY14 in Tea and 110 degrees at K7G9 near Canton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong ridge of high pressure persisted through the Central and Southern Plains at the start of September and brought hot temperatures to the region with highs in the 90s to around 100 degrees. An immense ridge of high pressure over the Central Plains and Mississippi Valley entrenched heat and high dewpoint air across the region, resulting in daily heat indices from 100 to around 120, with very little recovery during the overnight hours. One direct fatality occurred when an infant was left in a vehicle for an extended period of time during the day in Yankton.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Daily afternoon and evening heat indices ranged from 100 to 115 degrees with a maximum heat index of 115 degrees at Automated Weather Observing System site KYKN at Yankton on August 21.  A direct fatality occurred in Yankton on the third day of excessive heat when a child was left in a vehicle for an extended time. At the time of the incident, the heat index was 112 degrees with an air temperature of 100 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated storm developed in the axis of greatest instability consisting of 1500+ J/kg MLCAPE and 1000 J/kg DCAPE.  For a couple hours, a balance of shear and instability supported a long track of damaging winds and spotty large hail from near Miller southeast to near Beresford. Measured winds from 60 to 80 mph were common.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

Isolated storms formed within the instability axis ahead of a frontal zone in central South Dakota. With up to 2000 J/kg MLCAPE and 30-35 knots of deep-layer shear, a few storms took on brief supercell structures and produced large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two areas of thunderstorms brought severe weather to central and northeast South Dakota on the evening of July 25th. A warm front across northeastern South Dakota provided the focus for storms that generated at least one observed wind gust of 63 mph. A secondary area of thunderstorms developed over central South Dakota near a surface low-pressure system. The thunderstorms that developed over Hand County resulted in quarter-size hail, winds in excess of 60 mph, and a brief tornado that produced EF0 damage.

Several storms in an environment of 1500-2000 MLCAPE and around 40 knots of distributed deep-layer shear took on supercellular characteristics and produced hail up to baseball size in southwest Minnesota.

An outflow boundary from earlier day convection focused a gradient of instability and storm development with additional destabilization from heating by early afternoon. Boundary interactions from sluggish storms led to training of heavy rainfall cores and localized flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall damaged and partially washed out 437th Avenue. Property damage costs are rough estimates.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A heavy rain event in early August pushed the James River near Huron back into Minor Flood Stage for much of the month. Impacts were limited to minor agriculture flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The James River at Huron rose above flood stage on the morning of the 6th before cresting 0.41 ft above flood stage (11.41 ft) on the 19th. The river then fell below flood stage on the morning of the 27th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An expanding upper-level ridge across the southern and central U.S. pushed heat into the region, as moisture pooled just ahead of an advancing frontal zone.  The combination of heat and higher dewpoints resulted in afternoon and early evening heat indices from 100 to 115 degrees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A maximum heat index of 107 degrees was reached at the South Dakota State University Mesonet site BKMS2 near Brookings and personal weather station GW0796 at Elkton, as well as 106 degrees at Volga.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a strong mid-level jet streak pushed northeast ahead of an upper-level low pressure area in southwest South Dakota, numerous thunderstorms developed along a weak surface boundary during the mid to late afternoon hours in far eastern South Dakota.  While instability was somewhat limited at a maximum of around 1000 J/kg, a very low LCL and concentration of 0-1 km bulk shear supported the development of four brief tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm chaser witnessed a very brief touchdown in an open soybean field between 470th and 471st Avenue and 199th and 200th Street. The lack of damage indicators did not allow for a rating of this tornado.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An immense ridge of high pressure over the Central Plains and Mississippi Valley entrenched heat and high dewpoint air across the region, resulting in daily heat indices from 100 to around 120, with very little recovery during the overnight hours. One direct fatality occurred when an infant was left in a vehicle for an extended period of time during the day in Yankton.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Daily afternoon and evening heat indices ranged from 109 to 121 degrees with a maximum heat index of 121 degrees at a personal weather station near Canova on August 23, 115 degrees at a South Dakota Road Weather Information System site near Dolton on August 21, and 111 degrees at a South Dakota Road Weather Information System site near Montrose on both August 21 and 23.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An immense ridge of high pressure over the Central Plains and Mississippi Valley entrenched heat and high dewpoint air across the region, resulting in daily heat indices from 100 to around 120, with very little recovery during the overnight hours. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Daily afternoon and evening heat indices ranged from 100 to 112 degrees with a maximum heat index of 112 degrees at a personal weather station near Bonesteel and 108 degrees at a site near Fairfax, and 107 degrees at a South Dakota Road Weather Information site near Pickstown on August 23.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A late day frontal zone moved into an unstable airmass with up to 2000 J/kg MUCAPE and sparked several severe thunderstorms, which produced large hail up to golf ball size. In addition, a couple of brief tornadic spin ups occurred across Kingsbury County, but resulted in no damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado spun up initially around one-third mile southeast of the intersection of 202nd Street and 442nd Aveune. A video documented the tornado as it tracked east-northeast across open fields for around one mile before lifting just south of 202nd Street approximately one-half mile east of 443rd Avenue.  The tornado caused no known damage to incur a rating. The average path width was 20 yards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A late day frontal zone moved into an unstable airmass with up to 2000 J/kg MUCAPE and sparked several severe thunderstorms, which produced large hail up to golf ball size. In addition, a couple of brief tornadic spin ups occurred across Kingsbury County, but resulted in no damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A report was relayed through De Smet Fire of a brief tornado which touched down north of 201st Street just south of Mud Lake.  The tornado tracked eastward across open grass and marsh lands for around one-half mile before reaching the southern portion of Mud Lake where the tornado was observed pulling up water shortly before dissipating.  The average path width was 20 yards. The tornado did not cause any damage to allow designation of a rating.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong ridge of high pressure persisted through the Central and Southern Plains at the start of September and brought hot temperatures to the region with highs in the 90s to around 100 degrees. Dewpoint readings were somewhat lower than late August, mainly in the 60s, which kept the more extreme heat at bay.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Over a two-day period from the afternoon of September 2 through early evening on September 3, a maximum heat index of 102 degrees was reached at South Dakota Road Weather Information System site SD601 near Corsica.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snowmelt resultant flooding from April continued through May and into June along the very slowly draining James River in South Dakota. While flood levels continued to decrease from their crest in April, minor flooding persisted into June in some locations, slowly falling through the month. This flooding mainly impacted agricultural land.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding continued from May along the James River near Mitchell, SD with very slowly falling levels through the month. The peak river level was 1.15 feet above flood stage (18.15 ft) occurring early in the day on the 1st, before falling below flood stage early on the 14th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated storm developed in the axis of greatest instability consisting of 1500+ J/kg MLCAPE and 1000 J/kg DCAPE.  For a couple hours, a balance of shear and instability supported a long track of damaging winds and spotty large hail from near Miller southeast to near Beresford. Measured winds from 60 to 80 mph were common.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm wind gust of 75 mph was measured by a hand-held anemometer 1 mile west-northwest of Ethan.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few storms developed during the afternoon along a cold frontal boundary with up to 2000 J/kg available CAPE and 40 to 50 knots of effective shear.  Rotating storms produced large hail, and isolated very large hail across southwest Minnesota.

A few storms developed during the afternoon along a cold frontal boundary with up to 2000 J/kg available CAPE and 40 to 50 knots of effective shear.  Rotating storms produced large hail, and isolated very large hail across southwest Minnesota.

A strong upper-level wave across northeastern South Dakota ignited isolated afternoon thunderstorms near a cold front across south central South Dakota. Storms thrived and became briefly supercellular in a highly sheared and unstable environment while tracking eastward south of South Dakota Highway 46 into the early evening.  After a brief decrease in intensity entering Iowa, storms strengthened once again mid to late evening moving east of Cherokee generally along and south of Iowa Highway 3 with a variety of severe weather types.
Conditions averaged warm (3 to 5 degrees above normal) and dry (50-75 percent of normal) across southeast South Dakota during June and resulted in up to a slight worsening of drought conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate (D1) drought conditions worsened to severe (D2) conditions during early June, and then to extreme (D3) by the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Linear storms developed near a frontal boundary, moving across southeast South Dakota and into northwest Iowa and southwest Minnesota early on June 24. Within an environment of shear, a strong low-level jet, and strong DCAPE, some storms produced severe-level winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to three inch diameter branches were broken by thunderstorm wind gusts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed along a cold front brought severe weather to central South Dakota during the evening hours. A storm moved into Pierre and Fort Pierre, SD, producing hail. There were numerous reports of baseball-sized hail across the area, and even one larger than a softball, with an estimated diameter of 5.0 inches. The hailstone is the largest on record in Hughes County (4.0 in Pierre on 6/30/1997 and Blunt on 8/26/2018). Damage was done to vehicles, roofs, and other outside structures. The storm also produced a brief tornado about 11 miles south of Fort Pierre, just west of US Highway 83, around 6:45 pm CDT. These storms then moved south and east through the evening, producing large hail and damaging winds. A wind gust of 100 mph was recorded just west of Oacoma at 8:15 pm CDT via a weather station from the South Dakota State University's mesonet. Damage was done to power poles, trees, trailers, and other property in and around Oacoma.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: D2 Severe Drought, which began in mid June, continued into the early part of July. Beneficial rainfall of a quarter to a half inch on July 3rd helped ease drought conditions over eastern Lyman County. Additional rainfall on the 6th of July improved eastern Lyman County to D1 Moderate Drought by July 11th. Heavy rain exceeding an inch on July 19th further improved drought conditions over Buffalo County.

D2 Severe Drought, which began in mid June, continued into the early part of July. Beneficial rainfall of a quarter to a half inch on July 3rd helped ease drought conditions over eastern Lyman County. Additional rainfall on the 6th of July improved eastern Lyman County to D1 Moderate Drought by July 11th. Heavy rain exceeding an inch on July 19th further improved drought conditions over Buffalo County.

The combination of much above average temperatures and a prolonged period of mostly dry conditions allowed D2 Severe Drought to expand into eastern and southern Roberts, most of Grant, and the northeast corner of Codington County. High temperatures exceeded 100 degrees in a few locations on July 26th.

A storm system moved from the northern Rockies to the central Plains Thanksgiving Day and night, mostly producing snowfall across southwest South Dakota. Snowfall amounts of 2 to 5 inches were received over far southwest South Dakota.

Thunderstorms developed along a cold front brought severe weather to central South Dakota during the evening hours. A storm moved into Pierre and Fort Pierre, SD, producing hail. There were numerous reports of baseball-sized hail across the area, and even one larger than a softball, with an estimated diameter of 5.0 inches. The hailstone is the largest on record in Hughes County (4.0 in Pierre on 6/30/1997 and Blunt on 8/26/2018). Damage was done to vehicles, roofs, and other outside structures. The storm also produced a brief tornado about 11 miles south of Fort Pierre, just west of US Highway 83, around 6:45 pm CDT. These storms then moved south and east through the evening, producing large hail and damaging winds. A wind gust of 100 mph was recorded just west of Oacoma at 8:15 pm CDT via a weather station from the South Dakota State University's mesonet. Damage was done to power poles, trees, trailers, and other property in and around Oacoma.

The combination of much above average temperatures and a prolonged period of mostly dry conditions allowed D2 Severe Drought to expand into eastern and southern Roberts, most of Grant, and the northeast corner of Codington County. High temperatures exceeded 100 degrees in a few locations on July 26th.

A severe thunderstorm developed just northwest of Rapid City and slowly drifted north and east through portions of the Rapid City, Black Hawk, and Summerset areas; producing hail around quarter size and heavy rain. Brief heavy rain and large amounts of small hail blocked storm drains and caused minor flooding across parts of Rapid City, with elevated stream levels on Rapid Creek east of Rapid City.

A strong thunderstorm produced hail to penny size in Saint Onge and heavy rain across the northern foothills area. Runoff from the heavy rain caused significant flooding in Spearfish and across other portions of northern Lawrence County. Asphalt buckled and gravel washed off numerous roads from the flooding and damaged buildings.

A strong thunderstorm produced hail to penny size in Saint Onge and heavy rain across the northern foothills area. Runoff from the heavy rain caused significant flooding in Spearfish and across other portions of northern Lawrence County. Asphalt buckled and gravel washed off numerous roads from the flooding and damaged buildings.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rain caused considerable damage in Spearfish. Numerous roads flooded and asphalt buckled on portions of Main Street and Jackson Boulevard. The USGS gage, Spearfish Creek at Spearfish, recorded its fifth highest level since 1947 with a flow of 1900 cfs. Damage was reported at several locations, including the DC Booth National Fish Hatchery and a private apartment complex.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Cedar Creek near Presho briefly rose above the 12 foot flood stage during the morning of October 13th. The flooding was caused by 3 to 5 inches of heavy rainfall. The creek crested at 12.36 feet during the mid morning hours before falling below flood stage at 1:30 PM CDT. The flood waters impacted low-lying pasture lands.

An area of low pressure brought heavy snow to western and central Corson and Dewey County. Snowfall amounts of 3 to 7 inches were reported, with the highest amount measured eight miles south-southwest of Keldron. The snow caused travel delays and slowed harvest.

A significant early Fall storm system brought soaking rains to southern and western portions of South Dakota. Minor flooding occurred along the White River in Jackson County, near and downstream from Interior.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from an estimated three to four inches of rain over 24 hours in the Badlands caused minor flooding of the White River around Interior. Agricultural land along the White River was inundated and the water approached highway 44, north of the river. The White River near Interior river gage crested at 14.83 feet and 12,800 cfs at 745 AM MDT.

A multi-day strong autumn storm system brought widespread rainfall to the region, including as much as 4-7 inches to portions of central South Dakota. While much of this rainfall was absorbed into the antecedent dry soils, enough runoff occurred to push the Missouri River at Springfield into Minor Flood Stage for a short period given its already high cool season flow. Impacts during the non-recreational season at this gage location are minimal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Missouri River at Springfield, SD rose into Minor Flood Stage on October 12th cresting 0.24 ft above flood stage (10.24 ft) on October 14th. The river then fell back below flood stage by the morning of October 16th.

Heat and humidity developed across the area on Saturday September 2nd and continued through Monday, September 4th. Temperatures on Saturday exceeded 100 degrees across central and north central South Dakota, while northeast South Dakota experienced heat index values above 100 degrees. A peak heat index of 114 degrees was observed on a personal weather station 3 miles southwest of Onaka, while Pierre regional airport observed a high temperature of 103 degrees. A daily record of 101 degrees was set in Mobridge as well. |On Sunday, 100 degree heat and heat index values over 100 degrees were more broadly spread across central, north central and northeast South Dakota. The state mesonet station in Oacoma recorded a high temperature of 104 degrees, and the Automated Weather Observing System (AWOS) 3 miles east of Milbank recorded a maximum heat index of 107 degrees. Watertown set a new daily record of 101 degrees, while the record of 99 degrees was tied in Mobridge. |Again on Monday, a broad expanse of 100 plus degree heat and heat index values were observed from central to northeast South Dakota, with the highest heat index value of 110 degrees observed at the South Dakota Department of Transportation sensor in Mellette, while the state mesonet station 16 miles south southeast of Fort Pierre recorded a high of 103 degrees. |While this 3-day period experienced both abnormal heat and humidity, for the most part temperatures and heat index values dropped below 75 degrees for at least a few hours in the morning.

A thunderstorm became severe as it tracked across portions of southwest South Dakota. Large hail around quarter size was reported across portions of the southern Black Hills and adjacent plains to the east.
Runoff from heavy rain caused flooding across portions of southern Meade County. Several roads were flooded, with gravel washed off numerous roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rain inundated several county roads, including Antelope Creek Road, New Underwood Road, Brustel Road, and Elm Springs Road. Crop and pasture land were flooded, as well as low lying areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rain caused flooding across portions of Tripp County. Flash flooding developed; which inundated several county roads, overwhelmed culverts, and caused ponding of water in fields.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Five to eight inches of rain fell within two days, with three inches of rain falling in an hour during the afternoon of June 22. Numerous roads were covered with water including 228th Street, 325th Avenue, 326th Avenue, 328th Avenue, 290th Street, 291st Street, and 327th Street. Water filled Roosevelt Lake and inundated several fields and low lying areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rain caused flooding across portions of Ziebach County during the late evening and overnight hours. Flash flooding inundated several county roads, overwhelmed culverts, and caused ponding of water in fields.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four to six inches of rain fell in three hours between Red Elm and Dupree. The water quickly entered Bear Creek, which became about a quarter mile wide; inundating roads, fields, crops, and outbuildings. Water approached a residence near Red Elm and a home near Dupree. Water covered portions of 216th Avenue and 217th Avenue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rain caused flooding across portions of Ziebach County during the late evening and overnight hours. Flash flooding inundated several county roads, overwhelmed culverts, and caused ponding of water in fields.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The flash flooding transitioned to flooding as high flows continued on Bear Creek after four to six inches of rain fell in three hours between Red Elm and Dupree. Bear Creek was reported to be a quarter mile wide in spots; which inundated roads, fields, crops, and outbuildings. Water approached a residence near Red Elm and a home near Dupree. Water covered portions of 216th Avenue and 217th Avenue.

A weak low pressure system and cold front helped to generate strong storms in north central and south central South Dakota during the late afternoon and evening hours of September 4th. The strongest recorded wind gust with any of these storms was 70 mph on a personal weather station at a residence 7 miles south of Morristown, SD.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed several tree branches at a residence 8 miles northwest of Kenel, SD which sits just south of the North Dakota state line. Another residence just over the border had siding removed by these strong winds.

A slow-moving thunderstorm briefly became severe over central Pennington County into north central Custer County, producing hail up to golf ball size along with heavy rainfall. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow-moving thunderstorm briefly became severe over central Pennington County into north central Custer County, producing hail up to golf ball size along with heavy rainfall. The storm became nearly stationary just west of Hermosa and produced 2 to 5 inches of rainfall in less than 2 hours. Runoff from the heavy rainfall caused several roads to be inundated with water and debris west of Hermosa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rain caused flash flooding in Ghost Canyon and Box Canyon, and along Battle Creek and Grace Coolidge Creek, west of Hermosa. Ghost Canyon Road and Battle Creek Road were flooded with water and debris. Water and debris filled the ditches along Highway 40 and Highway 36.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow-moving thunderstorm briefly became severe over central Pennington County into north central Custer County, producing hail up to golf ball size along with heavy rainfall. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rain caused flash flooding in Ghost Canyon and Box Canyon, west of Hermosa. Ghost Canyon Road and Battle Creek Road were flooded with water and debris. Battle Creek and Grace Coolidge Creek overflowed their banks in several areas. Water and debris filled the ditches along Highway 40 and Highway 36. The Battle Creek at Hermosa river gage crested at 8.86 feet and 846 cfs at 915 PM MST.

A weak low pressure system and cold front helped to generate strong storms in north central and south central South Dakota during the late afternoon and evening hours of September 4th. The strongest recorded wind gust with any of these storms was 70 mph on a personal weather station at a residence 7 miles south of Morristown, SD.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed trees and power lines at a residence 5 miles south of Morristown. The power outage from wind damage to power lines extended back to the northwest as far as Keldron, SD.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe thunderstorms merged into a line as they moved into and across south central South Dakota. Large hail and wind gusts over 75 mph were reported across parts of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts were estimated at 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated large hail producing storm moved across the James valley during the early morning hours of Friday, September 29th. Ping pong sized hail was observed at a residence 1 mile west of Groton.
An isolated large hail producing storm moved across the James valley during the early morning hours of Friday, September 29th. Ping pong sized hail was observed at a residence 1 mile west of Groton.

Wildfires in northwest Canada produced copious amounts of smoke which followed a low pressure system down into the Dakotas on September 5th, and lingered into the day of the 6th. The combination of smoke and light rainfall resulted in visibility falling to as low as 1/4 mile in Redfield, 3/4 miles in Britton, 1 and 1/4 of a mile in Mobridge and Milbank, 1 1/2 miles in Pierre and Aberdeen,  and 2 miles in Watertown. The Air Quality Index (AQI) for these sites hit the unhealthy category, with an AQI of 166 in Aberdeen, 161 in Watertown and 170 in Pierre. Mobridge and Pierre recorded reduced visibility for around 36 hours, while stations in northeast South Dakota recorded reduced visibility for around 24 hours.

A long-lived supercell thunderstorm developed over the northern Black Hills and tracked eastward across west central South Dakota, producing large hail and wind gusts over 70 mph. Considerable damage to property was reported north of Rapid City as the storm passed through the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The combination of large hail and strong winds caused damage to property.

Considerable damage to property was reported north of Rapid City as the storm passed through the area.

A severe thunderstorm developed southeast of Rapid City and tracked east across portions of west central South Dakota. The storm produced large hail and eventually strong wind gusts as it tracked into south central South Dakota and merged into a line with other storms.

A severe thunderstorm developed southeast of Rapid City and tracked east across portions of west central South Dakota. The storm produced large hail and eventually strong wind gusts as it tracked into south central South Dakota and merged into a line with other storms.

A severe thunderstorm developed southeast of Rapid City and tracked east across portions of west central South Dakota. The storm produced large hail and eventually strong wind gusts as it tracked into south central South Dakota and merged into a line with other storms.
A thunderstorm briefly became severe as it tracked from central into eastern portions of Pennington County. The storm produced hail to quarter size from New Underwood to near Wasta.

The month of September started off with continued Severe Drought (D2) in far northeast South Dakota. Improvement through the course of the month was observed with D2 conditions falling off by September 26th. Rainfall for Sisseton was 3.57 inches for the month, or 1.12 inches above normal.

The month of September started off with continued Severe Drought (D2) in far northeast South Dakota. Improvement through the course of the month was observed with D2 conditions falling off by September 26th. Rainfall for Sisseton was 3.57 inches for the month, or 1.12 inches above normal.

A stalled front allowed for the generation of scattered afternoon thunderstorms. Two of these storms generated large hail, with a report of ping pong sized hail at a residence 6 miles west northwest of Okaton and golf ball sized hail 5 miles south of Hayes.

A stalled front allowed for the generation of scattered afternoon thunderstorms. Two of these storms generated large hail, with a report of ping pong sized hail at a residence 6 miles west northwest of Okaton and golf ball sized hail 5 miles south of Hayes.

The James River near Stratford opened the month still in flood at 14.83 feet, or 0.83 feet above flood stage. The gage fell below flood stage on September 8th, 147 days after initially going into flood. Flooding in September was confined to agricultural lands.

A supercell thunderstorm developed over the northern Black Hills and tracked northeast across portions of Butte and northern Meade Counties. The storm produced large hail to at least golf ball size, along with wind gusts around 60 mph.

A strong thunderstorm produced hail to penny size in Saint Onge and heavy rain across the northern foothills area. Runoff from the heavy rain caused significant flooding in Spearfish and across other portions of northern Lawrence County. Asphalt buckled and gravel washed off numerous roads from the flooding and damaged buildings.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rain caused considerable damage in Spearfish. Numerous roads flooded and asphalt buckled on portions of Main Street and Jackson Boulevard. The USGS gage, Spearfish Creek at Spearfish, recorded its fifth highest level since 1947 with a flow of 1900 cfs. Damage was reported at several locations, including the DC Booth National Fish Hatchery and a private apartment complex.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms tracking across eastern Oglala Lakota County produced a few wind gusts to around 60 mph.

Weak low pressure lifting into eastern South Dakota supported widespread thunderstorm activity in the afternoon. One of the stronger storms generated a short lived tornado 4 miles east northeast of the Poinsett Colony in Deuel county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A member of the public reported a brief tornado touchdown. No damage was noted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heat and humidity developed across the area on Saturday September 2nd and continued through Monday, September 4th. Temperatures on Saturday exceeded 100 degrees across central and north central South Dakota, while northeast South Dakota experienced heat index values above 100 degrees. A peak heat index of 114 degrees was observed on a personal weather station 3 miles southwest of Onaka, while Pierre regional airport observed a high temperature of 103 degrees. A daily record of 101 degrees was set in Mobridge as well. |On Sunday, 100 degree heat and heat index values over 100 degrees were more broadly spread across central, north central and northeast South Dakota. The state mesonet station in Oacoma recorded a high temperature of 104 degrees, and the Automated Weather Observing System (AWOS) 3 miles east of Milbank recorded a maximum heat index of 107 degrees. Watertown set a new daily record of 101 degrees, while the record of 99 degrees was tied in Mobridge. |Again on Monday, a broad expanse of 100 plus degree heat and heat index values were observed from central to northeast South Dakota, with the highest heat index value of 110 degrees observed at the South Dakota Department of Transportation sensor in Mellette, while the state mesonet station 16 miles south southeast of Fort Pierre recorded a high of 103 degrees. |While this 3-day period experienced both abnormal heat and humidity, for the most part temperatures and heat index values dropped below 75 degrees for at least a few hours in the morning.

With abnormal heat and humidity across the area for early September, isolated thunderstorms developed during the early morning hours. One of these storms generated a heat burst near the town of Redfield. The Automated Weather Observing System (AWOS) at the Redfield airport recorded a wind gust of 57 mph, with the associated winds causing some roofing material to get blown off a trailer 4 miles south of Redfield. This heat burst also resulted in a temperature jump from 73 degrees to 84 degrees in just 20 minutes at the AWOS.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew building material off a trailer and scattered it across the property.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long-lived severe thunderstorm over northeastern Wyoming moved into the central Black Hills before weakening, producing wind gusts to 60 mph in the Deerfield area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts were estimated at 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An overnight thunderstorm became severe over western Custer County and tracked east across most of the county before weakening. The storm produced large hail to golf ball size in the Argyle area.
A long-lived severe thunderstorm tracked from Wyoming across portions of the northern Black Hills area and southern Butte County, producing large hail and gusty winds.
A thunderstorm quickly became severe in the Rapid City area and tracked eastward onto the adjacent plains. Hail around quarter size accompanied the storm.

A long-lived supercell thunderstorm developed over the northern Black Hills and tracked eastward across west central South Dakota, producing large hail and wind gusts over 70 mph. Considerable damage to property was reported north of Rapid City as the storm passed through the area.

A severe thunderstorm developed southeast of Rapid City and tracked east across portions of west central South Dakota. The storm produced large hail and eventually strong wind gusts as it tracked into south central South Dakota and merged into a line with other storms.
An overnight thunderstorm became severe over western Custer County and tracked east across most of the county before weakening. The storm produced large hail to golf ball size in the Argyle area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to quarter size fell for about 20 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm developed over Rapid City, mostly affecting central and southern parts of the city. The storm produced hail to golf ball size in some areas.
A severe thunderstorm tracked southeast from Montana across western Harding County before weakening as it moved eastward. The storm produced hail to quarter size and gusty winds.
A thunderstorm produced a brief tornado southeast of Lemmon. No damage was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado briefly developed southeast of Lemmon. No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unseasonably warm and moist air contributed to the development of dense fog across southeast South Dakota, northwest Iowa, northeast Nebraska and southwest Minnesota on the morning of December 22.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Persistent and dense fog developed during the early and mid-morning hours of December 22. During this time, a multi-vehicle accident occurred along southbound Interstate 29 north of Beresford. A semi-truck failed to move over for vehicles responding to an earlier accident, and while attempting to avoid collision, rolled over and crushed a fire-rescue vehicle and hit an ambulance resulting in several (indirect) minor injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex storm impacted the Northern Plains in the days leading up to and through the Christmas holiday with widespread precipitation, wind, and hazardous conditions. The storm began as rain during the afternoon and evening of December 23, briefly pausing before a second round of rainfall surged northward early on December 24, with temperatures cold enough for snowfall from the James River westward. ||Cooler temperatures near the surface by early Christmas Day caused a widespread transition to freezing rain with light ice accumulation for areas east of the James River.  Farther west, very strong winds gusted to 40 to 55 mph on Christmas Day with heavy snow developing west of the James River Valley, which totaled from 5 to 15 inches. Snow rates diminished, as well as the wind by later Christmas Day. ||Travel was not recommended in areas west of the James River, and Interstate 90 was closed west of Mitchell from later afternoon on Christmas Day until mid-morning on December 26.  Power outages were sporadic and impacted several hundred customers generally along the I-29 corridor due to ice accumulation and strong wind on Christmas morning.||Several long-term NWS Cooperative Observer locations in southeast South Dakota recorded the highest 3-day precipitation total on record for December including:||White Lake  2.39|Chamberlain 5S  2.31|Academy 2NE  2.15|De Smet  1.95|Alexandria  1.89|Flandreau  1.80|Colton  1.79|Sioux Falls  1.76|Menno  1.71|Brookings  1.68|Howard  1.61.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow along with winds which gusted as high as 50 mph at the South Dakota Road Weather Information System at Kimball and 43 mph at the Automated Weather Observing System site K9V9 at Chamberlain,  led to persistent visibility of a quarter mile or less.  Snow accumulated to 10.2 inches 5 miles south of Chamberlain and 7.7 inches at Chamberlain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system developing close to the Gulf of Mexico lifted north and then west into southeast South Dakota, resulting in heavy precipitation and strong winds. While initially snow and freezing drizzle, warm and humid air pushing into the region from the east resulted in precipitation changing over to more widespread freezing rain and rain east of the Missouri river.||Atmospheric moisture associated with the system was significantly above normal for mid-winter. Moisture over the course of the 2-3 day event measured 2.82 inches in LaBolt, 2.68 inches in Milbank, 2.53 inches 6 miles north of Browns Valley and 2.44 inches 2 miles east of Sisseton. Daily records for moisture were set at the Sisseton Municipal Airport on both December 25th and December 26th at 1.43 and 1.17 inches respectively. ||The ice was just one component of a highly impactful winter storm that occurred during a major travel holiday. Winds topped out at 57 mph at the DOT station in Lake City, with numerous other reports of wind gusts at or above 50 mph. The combination of ice and strong winds resulted in numerous reports of tree damage and power interruptions. One NWS employee lost power to their house for 3 hours during the storm, while the northern portions of the Sisseton hills witnessed numerous line failures.  ||In addition to tree damage and power outages caused by this storm, travel was severely impacted by icy road conditions. Numerous Emergency Managers issued no travel advised for their counties, and several accidents were reported. Road conditions remained icy into Wednesday, December 27th, thanks to lingering light precipitation. This included a stretch of Interstate 29 which was blocked by a jackknifed semi during the evening commute.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service employee observed almost every tree in the town had experience storm damage in the form of downed limbs and branches across the town of Bowdle due to the ice and wind.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system developing close to the Gulf of Mexico lifted north and then west into southeast South Dakota, resulting in heavy precipitation and strong winds. While initially snow and freezing drizzle, warm and humid air pushing into the region from the east resulted in precipitation changing over to more widespread freezing rain and rain east of the Missouri, while along and just west of the Missouri, precipitation remained mainly in the form of snow. ||Snowfall amounts peaked in central South Dakota, with the COOP observer 7 miles east of Hayes measuring a foot of snow while just over 10 inches was observed 1 mile north northwest of Kennebec. The COOP observer for the city of Pierre measured snow at 7 inches. ||Wind gusts were also extreme, with a peak wind gusts of 63 mph observed by the Reliance Road Weather Information System (RWIS), 62 mph at the Mac's Corners RWIS, 61 mph at the Bowdle RWIS and 60 mph at the Herried RWIS. An additional 28 other weather stations recorded wind gusts in excess 50 mph. ||The combination of snow and strong winds resulted in blizzard conditions, with a quarter-mile visibility observed with the Pierre Regional Airport Automated Surface Observing System. Additionally, low visibility to near whiteout conditions were observed at many of the DOT weather stations in central and north central South Dakota. The impacts and scope of these blizzard conditions was highly dependent whether it was snowing at the time of strong winds, as there was little snowpack in the area prior to the storm. Additionally, blizzard conditions were also limited at times due to intermittent transitions back and forth between snow and freezing rain as the main precipitation type. ||The blizzard was just one component of a highly impactful winter storm that occurred during a major travel holiday. Travel was severely impacted due to the combination of freezing rain, heavy snow and/or blizzard conditions from December 25th through early on December 27th. Interstate 90 from Wall to Mitchell was closed for a period with this system starting on the afternoon of December 25th, and remained closed through the morning of December 26th. Even after the interstate opened, it was blocked by at least one semi rollover. In addition to the interstate closure, no travel advised was posted for many area highways by county emergency management and the DOT.

The COOP observer for the city of Pierre measured snow at 7 inches. ||Wind gusts were also extreme, with a peak wind gusts of 63 mph observed by the Reliance Road Weather Information System (RWIS), 62 mph at the Mac's Corners RWIS, 61 mph at the Bowdle RWIS and 60 mph at the Herried RWIS. An additional 28 other weather stations recorded wind gusts in excess 50 mph. ||The combination of snow and strong winds resulted in blizzard conditions, with a quarter-mile visibility observed with the Pierre Regional Airport Automated Surface Observing System. Additionally, low visibility to near whiteout conditions were observed at many of the DOT weather stations in central and north central South Dakota. The impacts and scope of these blizzard conditions was highly dependent whether it was snowing at the time of strong winds, as there was little snowpack in the area prior to the storm. Additionally, blizzard conditions were also limited at times due to intermittent transitions back and forth between snow and freezing rain as the main precipitation type. ||The blizzard was just one component of a highly impactful winter storm that occurred during a major travel holiday. Travel was severely impacted due to the combination of freezing rain, heavy snow and/or blizzard conditions from December 25th through early on December 27th. Interstate 90 from Wall to Mitchell was closed for a period with this system starting on the afternoon of December 25th, and remained closed through the morning of December 26th. Even after the interstate opened, it was blocked by at least one semi rollover. In addition to the interstate closure, no travel advised was posted for many area highways by county emergency management and the DOT.

A complex storm impacted the Northern Plains in the days leading up to and through the Christmas holiday with widespread precipitation, wind, and hazardous conditions. The storm began as rain during the afternoon and evening of December 23, briefly pausing before a second round of rainfall surged northward early on December 24, with temperatures cold enough for snowfall from the James River westward. ||Cooler temperatures near the surface by early Christmas Day caused a widespread transition to freezing rain with light ice accumulation for areas east of the James River.  Farther west, very strong winds gusted to 40 to 55 mph on Christmas Day with heavy snow developing west of the James River Valley, which totaled from 5 to 15 inches. Snow rates diminished, as well as the wind by later Christmas Day. ||Travel was not recommended in areas west of the James River, and Interstate 90 was closed west of Mitchell from later afternoon on Christmas Day until mid-morning on December 26.  Power outages were sporadic and impacted several hundred customers generally along the I-29 corridor due to ice accumulation and strong wind on Christmas morning.||Several long-term NWS Cooperative Observer locations in southeast South Dakota recorded the highest 3-day precipitation total on record for December including:||White Lake  2.39|Chamberlain 5S  2.31|Academy 2NE  2.15|De Smet  1.95|Alexandria  1.89|Flandreau  1.80|Colton  1.79|Sioux Falls  1.76|Menno  1.71|Brookings  1.68|Howard  1.61.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow along with winds which gusted as high as 50 mph at the South Dakota Road Weather Information System site 13 miles west of Platte and 42 mph near Wagner, led to persistent visibility of a quarter mile or less.  Snow accumulated to 11.4 inches near Academy and 7.3 inches at Platte.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A period of mixed freezing rain, sleet, and snow developed early on Christmas Day, leading to a brief ice accumulation up to a tenth of an inch which brought down several smaller branches around Flandreau. Snow and sleet accumulated to a little less than an inch at Flandreau.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system developing close to the Gulf of Mexico lifted north and then west into southeast South Dakota, resulting in heavy precipitation and strong winds. While initially snow and freezing drizzle, warm and humid air pushing into the region from the east resulted in precipitation changing over to more widespread freezing rain and rain east of the Missouri river.||Atmospheric moisture associated with the system was significantly above normal for mid-winter. Moisture over the course of the 2-3 day event measured 2.82 inches in LaBolt, 2.68 inches in Milbank, 2.53 inches 6 miles north of Browns Valley and 2.44 inches 2 miles east of Sisseton. Daily records for moisture were set at the Sisseton Municipal Airport on both December 25th and December 26th at 1.43 and 1.17 inches respectively. ||The ice was just one component of a highly impactful winter storm that occurred during a major travel holiday. Winds topped out at 57 mph at the DOT station in Lake City, with numerous other reports of wind gusts at or above 50 mph. The combination of ice and strong winds resulted in numerous reports of tree damage and power interruptions. One NWS employee lost power to their house for 3 hours during the storm, while the northern portions of the Sisseton hills witnessed numerous line failures.  ||In addition to tree damage and power outages caused by this storm, travel was severely impacted by icy road conditions. Numerous Emergency Managers issued no travel advised for their counties, and several accidents were reported. Road conditions remained icy into Wednesday, December 27th, thanks to lingering light precipitation. This included a stretch of Interstate 29 which was blocked by a jackknifed semi during the evening commute.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: National Weather Service personnel measured 0.25 inch radial ice accumulation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With the help of diurnal heating, synoptic winds were boosted during the afternoon behind a cold front which brought a few light showers of rain and snow to the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds were sustained from 25 to 30 mph at times, with gusts as high as 45 mph at South Dakota State University Mesonet site WTES2 near White Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex storm impacted the Northern Plains in the days leading up to and through the Christmas holiday with widespread precipitation, wind, and hazardous conditions. Snow accumulated to 6.0 inches at Wessington Springs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense low pressure system developed over the central Plains and only slowly moved east-northeast across the region during the Christmas holiday period. Heavy snow developed from south-central South Dakota, west-northwest into the northern Black Hills area. Strong winds on the plains produced blowing snow and blizzard conditions at times across much of the southwest into central South Dakota plains. Snowfall amounts of 6 to 15 inches were noted over a good portion of southwest and south-central South Dakota and the northern Black Hills area, with locally higher amounts. Snowfall amounts tailed off quite a bit into northwest South Dakota, especially far northwest South Dakota. Amounts were also less in downslope favored areas of the eastern/southeastern Black Hills and adjacent plains. Travel was very difficult to near impossible in some areas, with Interstate 90 east of Rapid City closed for an extended period on December 26th into the 27th.

One NWS employee lost power to their house for 3 hours during the storm, while the northern portions of the Sisseton hills witnessed numerous line failures.  ||In addition to tree damage and power outages caused by this storm, travel was severely impacted by icy road conditions. Numerous Emergency Managers issued no travel advised for their counties, and several accidents were reported. Road conditions remained icy into Wednesday, December 27th, thanks to lingering light precipitation. This included a stretch of Interstate 29 which was blocked by a jackknifed semi during the evening commute.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported an estimated 0.75 inches of flat ice, along with tree damage and falling branches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex storm impacted the Northern Plains in the days leading up to and through the Christmas holiday with widespread precipitation, wind, and hazardous conditions. The storm began as rain during the afternoon and evening of December 23, briefly pausing before a second round of rainfall surged northward early on December 24, with temperatures cold enough for snowfall from the James River westward. ||Cooler temperatures near the surface by early Christmas Day caused a widespread transition to freezing rain with light ice accumulation for areas east of the James River.  Farther west, very strong winds gusted to 40 to 55 mph on Christmas Day with heavy snow developing west of the James River Valley, which totaled from 5 to 15 inches. Snow rates diminished, as well as the wind by later Christmas Day. ||Travel was not recommended in areas west of the James River, and Interstate 90 was closed west of Mitchell from later afternoon on Christmas Day until mid-morning on December 26.  Power outages were sporadic and impacted several hundred customers generally along the I-29 corridor due to ice accumulation and strong wind on Christmas morning.||Several long-term NWS Cooperative Observer locations in southeast South Dakota recorded the highest 3-day precipitation total on record for December including:||White Lake  2.39|Chamberlain 5S  2.31|Academy 2NE  2.15|De Smet  1.95|Alexandria  1.89|Flandreau  1.80|Colton  1.79|Sioux Falls  1.76|Menno  1.71|Brookings  1.68|Howard  1.61.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall started just prior to daybreak and continued into early Christmas evening before mixing with and changing to a light freezing rain.  Snow was heavy at times between 0700 and 0900 CST with visibility briefly as low as 1/4 mile as winds gusted occasionally from 30 to 35 mph at the South Dakota State University Mesonet site 7 miles west-southwest of Beresford. Snow accumulated to 6.0 inches in Vermillion. Freezing rain resulted in less than one tenth of an inch of icing on elevated surfaces on Christmas evening.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense low pressure system developed over the central Plains and only slowly moved east-northeast across the region during the Christmas holiday period. Heavy snow developed from south-central South Dakota, west-northwest into the northern Black Hills area. Strong winds on the plains produced blowing snow and blizzard conditions at times across much of the southwest into central South Dakota plains. Snowfall amounts of 6 to 15 inches were noted over a good portion of southwest and south-central South Dakota and the northern Black Hills area, with locally higher amounts. Snowfall amounts tailed off quite a bit into northwest South Dakota, especially far northwest South Dakota. Amounts were also less in downslope favored areas of the eastern/southeastern Black Hills and adjacent plains. Travel was very difficult to near impossible in some areas, with Interstate 90 east of Rapid City closed for an extended period on December 26th into the 27th.

Gusty winds developed behind a cold front across much of western South Dakota. The strongest winds developed over far southwest South Dakota, where west to northwest winds of 30 to 45 mph and gusts to around 55 mph were recorded for a brief period during the early afternoon.

A strong cold front passed through western South Dakota late in the day, bringing gusty northwesterly winds to much of the area. The strongest winds developed over the Pennington County plains, where sustained winds of 30 to 45 mph and a few gusts of over 60 mph were recorded east of Rapid City.

An intense low pressure system developed over the central Plains and only slowly moved east-northeast across the region during the Christmas holiday period. Heavy snow developed from south-central South Dakota, west-northwest into the northern Black Hills area. Strong winds on the plains produced blowing snow and blizzard conditions at times across much of the southwest into central South Dakota plains. Snowfall amounts of 6 to 15 inches were noted over a good portion of southwest and south-central South Dakota and the northern Black Hills area, with locally higher amounts. Snowfall amounts tailed off quite a bit into northwest South Dakota, especially far northwest South Dakota. Amounts were also less in downslope favored areas of the eastern/southeastern Black Hills and adjacent plains. Travel was very difficult to near impossible in some areas, with Interstate 90 east of Rapid City closed for an extended period on December 26th into the 27th.

A strong low pressure system developing close to the Gulf of Mexico lifted north and then west into southeast South Dakota, resulting in heavy precipitation and strong winds. While initially snow and freezing drizzle, warm and humid air pushing into the region from the east resulted in precipitation changing over to more widespread freezing rain and rain east of the Missouri river.||Atmospheric moisture associated with the system was significantly above normal for mid-winter. Moisture over the course of the 2-3 day event measured 2.82 inches in LaBolt, 2.68 inches in Milbank, 2.53 inches 6 miles north of Browns Valley and 2.44 inches 2 miles east of Sisseton. Daily records for moisture were set at the Sisseton Municipal Airport on both December 25th and December 26th at 1.43 and 1.17 inches respectively. ||The ice was just one component of a highly impactful winter storm that occurred during a major travel holiday. Winds topped out at 57 mph at the DOT station in Lake City, with numerous other reports of wind gusts at or above 50 mph. The combination of ice and strong winds resulted in numerous reports of tree damage and power interruptions. One NWS employee lost power to their house for 3 hours during the storm, while the northern portions of the Sisseton hills witnessed numerous line failures.  ||In addition to tree damage and power outages caused by this storm, travel was severely impacted by icy road conditions. Numerous Emergency Managers issued no travel advised for their counties, and several accidents were reported. Road conditions remained icy into Wednesday, December 27th, thanks to lingering light precipitation. This included a stretch of Interstate 29 which was blocked by a jackknifed semi during the evening commute.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported 0.25 inches of ice accumulation in the town of Roslyn.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex storm impacted the Northern Plains in the days leading up to and through the Christmas holiday with widespread precipitation, wind, and hazardous conditions. The storm began as rain during the afternoon and evening of December 23, briefly pausing before a second round of rainfall surged northward early on December 24, with temperatures cold enough for snowfall from the James River westward. ||Cooler temperatures near the surface by early Christmas Day caused a widespread transition to freezing rain with light ice accumulation for areas east of the James River.  Farther west, very strong winds gusted to 40 to 55 mph on Christmas Day with heavy snow developing west of the James River Valley, which totaled from 5 to 15 inches. Snow rates diminished, as well as the wind by later Christmas Day. ||Travel was not recommended in areas west of the James River, and Interstate 90 was closed west of Mitchell from later afternoon on Christmas Day until mid-morning on December 26.  Power outages were sporadic and impacted several hundred customers generally along the I-29 corridor due to ice accumulation and strong wind on Christmas morning.||Several long-term NWS Cooperative Observer locations in southeast South Dakota recorded the highest 3-day precipitation total on record for December including:||White Lake  2.39|Chamberlain 5S  2.31|Academy 2NE  2.15|De Smet  1.95|Alexandria  1.89|Flandreau  1.80|Colton  1.79|Sioux Falls  1.76|Menno  1.71|Brookings  1.68|Howard  1.61.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A period of mixed freezing rain and sleet developed early on Christmas Day, leading to a brief ice accumulation up to a tenth of an inch which brought down several smaller branches around Sioux Falls.  Precipitation changed to rain and drizzle during the afternoon, but falling temperatures at night led to some additional freezing drizzle later into Christmas night. Snow and sleet accumulated to around an inch at Sioux Falls and Hartford, and up to 2.9 inches at Colton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rapid snowmelt from a few day period of well above normal temperatures focused on the 11-13th of April resulted in moderate to locally major flooding through much of the James River Basin. Some areas north of I-90 lost between 2 to 5 inches of snow water equivalent (SWE) from the first to second week of April, but antecedent drought conditions resulted in only a percentage of this water actually making it into the river system.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The James River near Forestburg, SD rose above flood stage on the morning of April 15th before cresting around 3.35 feet above flood stage (15.35 feet) on April 26th, which is in the Moderate category. The river was above Minor flood stage at this location for 16 days and above Moderate stage for 10 days. Flooding continued into May.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper-level wave moving through southwest flow aloft, acting on an environment with 1000 to 1500 J/kg of MUCAPE in the vicinity of a surface trough, resulted in scattered thunderstorms across far southeast South Dakota, far northeast Nebraska, northwest Iowa, and southwest Minnesota during the evening of April 19th. The strongest storms produced severe hail ranging from 1.0 to 1.5 inches in diameter.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail fell in Elk Point.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong wind gradient around a large upper level circulation resulted in widespread winds of 30 to 40 mph and gusts from 45 to 65 mph east of the James River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Northwest winds gusted as high as 53 mph at the South Dakota Road Weather Information System site SD582 3 miles north-northwest of Wolsey and 46 mph at SD515 3 miles east of Cavour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rapid snowmelt from a few day period of well above normal temperatures focused on the 11-13th of April resulted in moderate to locally major flooding through much of the James River Basin. Some areas north of I-90 lost between 2 to 5 inches of snow water equivalent (SWE) from the first to second week of April, but antecedent drought conditions resulted in only a percentage of this water actually making it into the river system.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The James River near Huron, SD rose above flood stage around midday on April 12th before cresting around 4.85 feet above flood stage (15.85 feet) on April 24th, which is in the Major category. The river was above Minor flood stage at this location for 19 days, above Moderate stage for 17 days, and above Major stage for 8 days. Flooding continued into May.
